We present SVA Generator, a data-centric framework that translates natural-language SVA Descriptions (SVADs) into executable SVAs. It uses AST-grounded constraint injection and an automated supervision pipeline that enforces structural consistency and reduces hallucinations via de-duplication and constraint checks. To enable rigorous evaluation, we introduce a benchmark suite stratified by AST depth and use formal property equivalence checking to quantify semantic correctness separately from syntax validity, by checking mutual implication between the generated and reference properties under the same clocking and environment assumptions. Across all difficulty tiers, SVA Generator achieves comparable Syntax Pass Rate (SPR) to strong general LLM baselines, while delivering substantially higher Semantic Equivalence Rate (SER) on deeper tiers: +24.5 pp on D2, +26.0 pp on D3, and +17.5 pp on D4 relative to the best-performing general LLM, corresponding to a +22.7 pp SER improvement on average over D2--D4. These results highlight that high-fidelity data construction and depth-stratified benchmarking are key to reliable, semantics-preserving SVA generation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SVA Generator, a framework designed to translate natural-language descriptions into SystemVerilog Assertions (SVAs) with higher semantic correctness and syntax validity compared to general-purpose LLMs.
AI.motivation: Functional verification for integrated circuits is costly, and drafting SystemVerilog Assertions manually is cumbersome and error-prone. Current LLMs struggle due to limited domain-specific training data.
AI.method: The authors developed SVA Generator, leveraging AST-grounded constraints and automated supervision to improve consistency and accuracy. It employs a benchmark suite and formal equivalence checking for rigorous evaluation.
AI.result: SVA Generator achieves comparable syntax validity rates as standard LLMs while significantly outperforming in semantic correctness across higher complexity tiers (D2-D4), with average SER improvements of +22.7 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: High-quality, domain-specific data and depth-tracked benchmarking are essential for reliable generation of semantically correct SystemVerilog Assertions using machine learning frameworks.

title: Complexity of Consistency Testing for the Release-Acquire Semantics
comment: A shorter version has been accepte at FM 2026 - the 27th International Symposium on Formal Methods
summary:
In a seminal work, Gibbons and Korach studied the complexity of deciding whether an observed sequence of reads and writes of a multi-threaded program admits a sequentially consistent interleaving. They showed the problem to be NP-hard even under strong syntactic restrictions. More recently, Chakraborty et al. considered the problem for weak memory models and proved that NP-hardness remains even when the number of threads, the number of memory locations, and the value domain are all bounded.
  In this paper we revisit the problem for the release-acquire variants of the C11 memory model. Our main positive result is that consistency testing can be done in polynomial-time when each memory location is written by at most one thread (multiple readers are allowed). Notably, this restriction is already NP-hard for sequential consistency. We complement this upper bound with tight hardness results: the problem is NP-hard when two threads may write to the same location, and allowing three writers per location rules out 2^{o(k)}.n^{O(1)} algorithms under the Exponential Time Hypothesis, where k denotes the number of threads, and n the number of memory operations.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes consistency testing in the C11 memory model under specific conditions—finding polynomial-time solutions for single-thread writes while confirming NP-hardness when multiple threads write to the same location.
AI.motivation: Address the unresolved complexity challenges in verifying consistency under the C11 memory model, a critical operation in multi-threaded programming.
AI.method: Examines the problem with both theoretical and computational approaches by imposing constraints—e.g., single-thread writes versus multi-thread writes—to evaluate consistency behavior.
AI.result: Polynomial-time consistency testing is achievable with one-thread write restrictions. NP-hardness persists when two or more threads write to the same memory location, and exponential complexity emerges with three or more writers under specific assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Efficient solutions exist under restricted writing conditions, but consistent behaviors face hardness with more writing threads, emphasizing the intricacy of weak memory model verification.

title: ADD for Multi-Bit Image Watermarking
summary: As generative models enable rapid creation of high-fidelity images, societal concerns about misinformation and authenticity have intensified. A promising remedy is multi-bit image watermarking, which embeds a multi-bit message into an image so that a verifier can later detect whether the image is generated by someone and further identify the source by decoding the embedded message. Existing approaches often fall short in capacity, resilience to common image distortions, and theoretical justification. To address these limitations, we propose ADD (Add, Dot, Decode), a multi-bit image watermarking method with two stages: learning a watermark to be linearly combined with the multi-bit message and added to the image, and decoding through inner products between the watermarked image and the learned watermark. On the standard MS-COCO benchmark, we demonstrate that for the challenging task of 48-bit watermarking, ADD achieves 100\% decoding accuracy, with performance dropping by at most 2\% under a wide range of image distortions, substantially smaller than the 14\% average drop of state-of-the-art methods. In addition, ADD achieves substantial computational gains, with 2-fold faster embedding and 7.4-fold faster decoding than the fastest existing method. We further provide a theoretical analysis explaining why the learned watermark and the corresponding decoding rule are effective.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ADD, a novel multi-bit image watermarking method offering high decoding accuracy and resilience against distortions, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in speed and robustness.
AI.motivation: Concerns over misinformation and image authenticity due to generative models necessitate robust watermarking techniques to ensure source verification and prevent misuse.
AI.method: The ADD method uses two stages: 1) learning a watermark that incorporates a multi-bit message and adding it to an image, and 2) decoding the watermark by calculating inner products between the image and the learned watermark.
AI.result: ADD achieves 100% decoding accuracy for 48-bit watermarking with a minimal performance drop (at most 2%) under distortions, outperforming existing methods, which suffer a 14% average drop. It also performs embedding 2x faster and decoding 7.4x faster than the current fastest method.
AI.conclusion: ADD provides a theoretically sound and computationally efficient approach for robust multi-bit image watermarking, addressing shortcomings of prior methods in capacity, distortion resilience, and speed.

title: PatchRecall: Patch-Driven Retrieval for Automated Program Repair
summary: Retrieving the correct set of files from a large codebase is a crucial step in Automated Program Repair (APR). High recall is necessary to ensure that the relevant files are included, but simply increasing the number of retrieved files introduces noise and degrades efficiency. To address this tradeoff, we propose PatchRecall, a hybrid retrieval approach that balances recall with conciseness. Our method combines two complementary strategies: (1) codebase retrieval, where the current issue description is matched against the codebase to surface potentially relevant files, and (2) history-based retrieval, where similar past issues are leveraged to identify edited files as candidate targets. Candidate files from both strategies are merged and reranked to produce the final retrieval set. Experiments on SWE-Bench demonstrate that PatchRecall achieves higher recall without significantly increasing retrieved file count, enabling more effective APR.

AI.tldr: PatchRecall enhances file retrieval for Automated Program Repair by balancing recall and efficiency using a hybrid strategy.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge in Automated Program Repair of retrieving relevant files from large codebases while balancing recall and efficiency.
AI.method: PatchRecall employs a hybrid approach combining codebase retrieval (matching issue descriptions with the codebase) and history-based retrieval (leveraging past issues/edit histories). It then merges and reranks retrieved candidates.
AI.result: PatchRecall achieves higher recall on SWE-Bench without significantly increasing the number of retrieved files.
AI.conclusion: The method improves file retrieval for APR, effectively enhancing recall without compromising efficiency.

title: MoRI: Mixture of RL and IL Experts for Long-Horizon Manipulation Tasks
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) and Imitation Learning (IL) are the standard frameworks for policy acquisition in manipulation. While IL offers efficient policy derivation, it suffers from compounding errors and distribution shift. Conversely, RL facilitates autonomous exploration but is frequently hindered by low sample efficiency and the high cost of trial and error. Since existing hybrid methods often struggle with complex tasks, we introduce Mixture of RL and IL Experts (MoRI). This system dynamically switches between IL and RL experts based on the variance of expert actions to handle coarse movements and fine-grained manipulations. MoRI employs an offline pre-training stage followed by online fine-tuning to accelerate convergence. To maintain exploration safety and minimize human intervention, the system applies IL-based regularization to the RL component. Evaluation across four complex real-world tasks shows that MoRI achieves an average success rate of 97.5% within 2 to 5 hours of fine-tuning. Compared to baseline RL algorithms, MoRI reduces human intervention by 85.8% and shortens convergence time by 21%, demonstrating its capability in robotic manipulation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MoRI, a framework combining Reinforcement Learning and Imitation Learning to efficiently train policies for complex robotic manipulation tasks while reducing human involvement and training time.
AI.motivation: Existing RL and IL methods struggle with balancing efficiency and robustness in tackling complex robotic manipulation tasks, motivating the need for a better hybrid approach.
AI.method: MoRI alternates between RL and IL based on action variance and incorporates offline pre-training, fine-tuning, and IL-based regularization to ensure safety and efficiency in exploration.
AI.result: MoRI achieves a success rate of 97.5% on real-world tasks, reduces human intervention by 85.8%, and cuts convergence time by 21% compared to baseline RL algorithms.
AI.conclusion: MoRI demonstrates its effectiveness and efficiency in robotic manipulation by addressing the limitations of compounding errors in IL and low sample efficiency in RL.

title: Weird Generalization is Weirdly Brittle
summary: Weird generalization is a phenomenon in which models fine-tuned on data from a narrow domain (e.g. insecure code) develop surprising traits that manifest even outside that domain (e.g. broad misalignment)-a phenomenon that prior work has highlighted as a critical safety concern. Here, we present an extended replication study of key weird generalization results across an expanded suite of models and datasets. We confirm that surprising (and dangerous) traits can emerge under certain circumstances, but we find that weird generalization is exceptionally brittle: it emerges only for specific models on specific datasets, and it vanishes under simple training-time, prompt-based interventions. We find that the most effective interventions provide prompt context that makes the generalized behavior the expected behavior. However, we show that even very generic interventions that do not anticipate specific generalized traits can still be effective in mitigating weird generalization's effects. Our findings thus help clarify the nature of the safety threat that weird generalization poses and point toward an easily implemented set of solutions.

AI.tldr: Weird generalization, a phenomenon where models show unexpected traits outside their training domain, is critically examined. While dangerous traits occasionally emerge, they are brittle and can often be mitigated by simple interventions.
AI.motivation: Investigate the safety threat posed by weird generalization, where models trained in narrow domains exhibit surprising misaligned behaviors beyond those domains.
AI.method: Conducted an extended replication study across diverse models/datasets to analyze weird generalization. Tested training-time and prompt-based interventions to mitigate its effects.
AI.result: Weird generalization was found to be brittle, emerging only under specific conditions. Training-time and generic prompt-based interventions significantly reduce its impact.
AI.conclusion: Weird generalization presents a safety risk but can be mitigated through effective, simple interventions, pointing toward practical solutions to address this concern.

title: Agentic Exploration of PDE Spaces using Latent Foundation Models for Parameterized Simulations
summary: Flow physics and more broadly physical phenomena governed by partial differential equations (PDEs), are inherently continuous, high-dimensional and often chaotic in nature. Traditionally, researchers have explored these rich spatiotemporal PDE solution spaces using laboratory experiments and/or computationally expensive numerical simulations. This severely limits automated and large-scale exploration, unlike domains such as drug discovery or materials science, where discrete, tokenizable representations naturally interface with large language models. We address this by coupling multi-agent LLMs with latent foundation models (LFMs), a generative model over parametrised simulations, that learns explicit, compact and disentangled latent representations of flow fields, enabling continuous exploration across governing PDE parameters and boundary conditions. The LFM serves as an on-demand surrogate simulator, allowing agents to query arbitrary parameter configurations at negligible cost. A hierarchical agent architecture orchestrates exploration through a closed loop of hypothesis, experimentation, analysis and verification, with a tool-modular interface requiring no user support. Applied to flow past tandem cylinders at Re = 500, the framework autonomously evaluates over 1,600 parameter-location pairs and discovers divergent scaling laws: a regime-dependent two-mode structure for minimum displacement thickness and a robust linear scaling for maximum momentum thickness, with both landscapes exhibiting a dual-extrema structure that emerges at the near-wake to co-shedding regime transition. The coupling of the learned physical representations with agentic reasoning establishes a general paradigm for automated scientific discovery in PDE-governed systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework combining latent foundation models (LFMs) and multi-agent language models (LLMs) to autonomously explore and analyze spatiotemporal PDE solutions, focusing on fluid dynamics around tandem cylinders.
AI.motivation: The work aims to enable automated and large-scale exploration of complex physical phenomena governed by PDEs, overcoming limitations of expensive numerical simulations and laboratory experiments.
AI.method: This method involves LFMs to generate compact latent representations of flow fields and a hierarchical agent architecture to autonomously navigate hypothesis and experimentation loops using negligible-cost simulations.
AI.result: Using the framework at Re = 500, new scaling laws and structural insights into flow fields around tandem cylinders are revealed, with dual-extrema structures emerging in both minimum displacement and maximum momentum thickness landscapes.
AI.conclusion: The research demonstrates the potential of coupling LFMs and agentic reasoning to establish a scalable paradigm for scientific discovery in PDE-governed systems.

title: CAGE: Bridging the Accuracy-Aesthetics Gap in Educational Diagrams via Code-Anchored Generative Enhancement
summary: Educational diagrams -- labeled illustrations of biological processes, chemical structures, physical systems, and mathematical concepts -- are essential cognitive tools in K-12 instruction. Yet no existing method can generate them both accurately and engagingly. Open-source diffusion models produce visually rich images but catastrophically garble text labels. Code-based generation via LLMs guarantees label correctness but yields visually flat outputs. Closed-source APIs partially bridge this gap but remain unreliable and prohibitively expensive at educational scale. We quantify this accuracy-aesthetics dilemma across all three paradigms on 400 K-12 diagram prompts, measuring both label fidelity and visual quality through complementary automated and human evaluation protocols. To resolve it, we propose CAGE (Code-Anchored Generative Enhancement): an LLM synthesizes executable code producing a structurally correct diagram, then a diffusion model conditioned on the programmatic output via ControlNet refines it into a visually polished graphic while preserving label fidelity. We also introduce EduDiagram-2K, a collection of 2,000 paired programmatic-stylized diagrams enabling this pipeline, and present proof-of-concept results and a research agenda for the multimedia community.

AI.tldr: This paper highlights the challenge of generating accurate and visually engaging educational diagrams and proposes a hybrid method combining LLMs and diffusion models to resolve label fidelity and aesthetic quality issues.
AI.motivation: Educational diagrams are critical for K-12 learning, but current methods either compromise diagram label accuracy or visual engagement, limiting their utility in education.
AI.method: The authors developed CAGE, a method where an LLM generates code for structurally correct diagrams, and a diffusion model refines these into visually appealing versions via ControlNet, while ensuring label fidelity.
AI.result: The study proposes a new dataset, EduDiagram-2K, and demonstrates that the hybrid pipeline overcomes the accuracy-aesthetic tradeoff, achieving better results in both dimensions based on evaluations.
AI.conclusion: By combining the strengths of code-based and diffusion model approaches, CAGE shows promise for generating educational diagrams at scale, opening new possibilities for multimedia-enhanced learning.

title: Explainable Human Activity Recognition: A Unified Review of Concepts and Mechanisms
summary: Human activity recognition (HAR) has become a key component of intelligent systems for healthcare monitoring, assistive living, smart environments, and human-computer interaction. Although deep learning has substantially improved HAR performance on multivariate sensor data, the resulting models often remain opaque, limiting trust, reliability, and real-world deployment. Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) has therefore emerged as a critical direction for making HAR systems more transparent and human-centered. This paper presents a comprehensive review of explainable HAR methods across wearable, ambient, physiological, and multimodal sensing settings. We introduce a unified perspective that separates conceptual dimensions of explainability from algorithmic explanation mechanisms, reducing ambiguities in prior surveys. Building on this distinction, we present a mechanism-centric taxonomy of XAI-HAR methods covering major explanation paradigms. The review examines how these methods address the temporal, multimodal, and semantic complexities of HAR, and summarize their interpretability objectives, explanation targets, and limitations. In addition, we discuss current evaluation practices, highlight key challenges in achieving reliable and deployable XAI-HAR, and outline directions toward trustworthy activity recognition systems that better support human understanding and decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews explainable methods for human activity recognition (HAR) and presents a taxonomy for better understanding and evaluation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of transparency and trust in HAR systems by incorporating explainable artificial intelligence (XAI).
AI.method: The paper introduces a unified perspective and a mechanism-centric taxonomy to organize XAI-HAR methods, addressing complexities in HAR and summarizing interpretability goals.
AI.result: Key challenges and current evaluation practices in XAI-HAR are identified, along with a discussion of methods to enhance their reliability and real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: The study underscores the importance of developing trustworthy XAI-HAR systems for better human understanding and decision-making, marking a significant step towards their practical use.

title: Heterogeneous Connectivity in Sparse Networks: Fan-in Profiles, Gradient Hierarchy, and Topological Equilibria
summary: Profiled Sparse Networks (PSN) replace uniform connectivity with deterministic, heterogeneous fan-in profiles defined by continuous, nonlinear functions, creating neurons with both dense and sparse receptive fields. We benchmark PSN across four classification datasets spanning vision and tabular domains, input dimensions from 54 to 784, and network depths of 2--3 hidden layers. At 90% sparsity, all static profiles, including the uniform random baseline, achieve accuracy within 0.2-0.6% of dense baselines on every dataset, demonstrating that heterogeneous connectivity provides no accuracy advantage when hub placement is arbitrary rather than task-aligned. This result holds across sparsity levels (80-99.9%), profile shapes (eight parametric families, lognormal, and power-law), and fan-in coefficients of variation from 0 to 2.5. Internal gradient analysis reveals that structured profiles create a 2-5x gradient concentration at hub neurons compared to the ~1x uniform distribution in random baselines, with the hierarchy strength predicted by fan-in coefficient of variation ($r = 0.93$). When PSN fan-in distributions are used to initialise RigL dynamic sparse training, lognormal profiles matched to the equilibrium fan-in distribution consistently outperform standard ERK initialisation, with advantages growing on harder tasks, achieving +0.16% on Fashion-MNIST ($p = 0.036$, $d = 1.07$), +0.43% on EMNIST, and +0.49% on Forest Cover. RigL converges to a characteristic fan-in distribution regardless of initialisation. Starting at this equilibrium allows the optimiser to refine weights rather than rearrange topology. Which neurons become hubs matters more than the degree of connectivity variance, i.e., random hub placement provides no advantage, while optimisation-driven placement does.

AI.tldr: Profiled Sparse Networks (PSNs) use deterministic heterogeneous connectivity profiles and show performance close to dense baselines, with structured initialization benefiting dynamic sparse training strategies like RigL.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to investigate whether deterministic, heterogeneous connectivity profiles in sparse networks offer any accuracy advantage over uniform random baselines, and how they influence neural network optimization and performance.
AI.method: The authors benchmark PSNs on four datasets with varying input dimensions and network depths, compare different sparsity levels and connectivity profiles, and analyze gradient concentration effects. They also test RigL sparse training performance with structured PSN initialization.
AI.result: PSNs with structured profiles achieve accuracy within 0.2-0.6% of dense baselines, regardless of profile shapes, sparsity levels, or fan-in variation. RigL training with lognormal PSN initialization consistently outperforms ERK initialization, especially on harder tasks.
AI.conclusion: Heterogeneous connectivity provides no accuracy advantage unless hubs are optimally placed via optimization. Structured initializations like PSNs can enhance training in dynamic sparse networks.

title: Duration-Informed Workload Scheduler
summary:
High-performance computing systems are complex machines whose behaviour is governed by the correct functioning of its many subsystems. Among these, the workload scheduler has a crucial impact on the timely execution of the jobs continuously submitted to the computing resources. Making high-quality scheduling decisions is contingent on knowing the duration of submitted jobs before their execution--a non-trivial task for users that can be tackled with Machine Learning.
  In this work, we devise a workload scheduler enhanced with a duration prediction module built via Machine Learning. We evaluate its effectiveness and show its performance using workload traces from a Tier-0 supercomputer, demonstrating a decrease in mean waiting time across all jobs of around 11%. Lower waiting times are directly connected to better quality of service from the users' point of view and higher turnaround from the system's perspective.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a Machine Learning-based workload scheduler for High-performance computing systems to predict job durations, reducing mean waiting time by 11%.
AI.motivation: High-performance computing systems rely heavily on efficient workload scheduling to ensure timely execution of jobs, but predicting job durations before execution is challenging.
AI.method: Integrating a Machine Learning-based duration prediction module into the workload scheduler and evaluating its impact using traces from a Tier-0 supercomputer.
AI.result: The proposed scheduler reduces mean waiting time for submitted jobs by an average of 11%, improving user quality of service and system throughput.
AI.conclusion: Machine Learning can enhance workload scheduling by predicting job durations, leading to more efficient resource utilization and improved experience for users and system operators.

title: Technology solutions targeting the performance of gen-AI inference in resource constrained platforms
summary: The rise of generative AI workloads, particularly language model inference, is intensifying on/off-chip memory pressure. Multimodal inputs such as video streams or images and downstream applications like Question Answering (QA) and analysis over large documents incur long context lengths, requiring caching of massive Key and Value states of the previous tokens. Even a low degree of concurrent inference serving on resource-constrained devices, like mobiles, can further add to memory capacity pressure and runtime memory management complexity. In this paper, we evaluate the performance implications of two emerging technology solutions to alleviate the memory pressure in terms of both capacity and bandwidth using a hierarchical roofline-based analytical performance model. For large models (e.g., 13B parameters) and context lengths, we investigate the performance implications of High Bandwidth Storage (HBS) and outline bandwidth/latency requirements to achieve an acceptable throughput for interactivity. For small models (e.g., 1B parameters), we evaluate the merit of a bonded global buffer memory chiplet and propose how to best utilize it.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the memory challenges presented by generative AI tasks and evaluates emerging technologies to mitigate memory pressure via analytical modeling.
AI.motivation: The increasing usage of generative AI with large context lengths and multimodal inputs creates significant memory pressure, particularly in resource-constrained scenarios like mobile devices.
AI.method: The authors use a hierarchical roofline-based analytical performance model to evaluate High Bandwidth Storage (HBS) for large models and a global buffer memory chiplet for small models.
AI.result: For large models, HBS shows promise, with bandwidth and latency outlined for optimal interactivity. For small models, a bonded global buffer chiplet is suggested with strategies for effective utilization.
AI.conclusion: Emerging memory technologies like HBS and buffer memory chiplets could alleviate both capacity and bandwidth issues in generative AI, improving performance and interactivity.

title: Last-Iterate Convergence of Randomized Kaczmarz and SGD with Greedy Step Size
summary: We study last-iterate convergence of SGD with greedy step size over smooth quadratics in the interpolation regime, a setting which captures the classical Randomized Kaczmarz algorithm as well as other popular iterative linear system solvers. For these methods, we show that the $t$-th iterate attains an $O(1/t^{3/4})$ convergence rate, addressing a question posed by Attia, Schliserman, Sherman, and Koren, who gave an $O(1/t^{1/2})$ guarantee for this setting. In the proof, we introduce the family of stochastic contraction processes, whose behavior can be described by the evolution of a certain deterministic eigenvalue equation, which we analyze via a careful discrete-to-continuous reduction.

AI.tldr: This paper studies last-iterate convergence of SGD with greedy step size for smooth quadratics in the interpolation regime, achieving an improved $O(1/t^{3/4})$ rate compared to the previous $O(1/t^{1/2})$.
AI.motivation: Address an open question about improving convergence guarantees in SGD under the interpolation regime, specifically for methods related to stochastic iterative linear solvers.
AI.method: Introduced stochastic contraction processes and analyzed their evolution via a deterministic eigenvalue equation, employing a discrete-to-continuous reduction strategy.
AI.result: Derived a stronger convergence rate of $O(1/t^{3/4})$ for the $t$-th iterate, surpassing the previously established $O(1/t^{1/2})$ rate.
AI.conclusion: The findings improve theoretical guarantees for convergence rates in SGD under certain smooth quadratic conditions, enhancing understanding of greedy step size methods.

title: SWE-Shepherd: Advancing PRMs for Reinforcing Code Agents
summary: Automating real-world software engineering tasks remains challenging for large language model (LLM)-based agents due to the need for long-horizon reasoning over large, evolving codebases and making consistent decisions across interdependent actions. Existing approaches typically rely on static prompting strategies or handcrafted heuristics to select actions such as code editing, file navigation, and test execution, but they lack fine-grained feedback on intermediate decisions. This leads to inefficient exploration, error propagation, and brittle solution trajectories. To address this limitation, we propose SWE-Shepherd, a framework that introduces Process Reward Models (PRMs) to provide dense, step-level supervision for repository-level code agents. Using trajectories from SWE-Bench, we construct an action-level reward dataset and train a lightweight reward model on a base LLM to estimate the usefulness of intermediate actions. During inference, the PRM evaluates candidate actions and guides the agent toward higher-reward decisions without requiring full reinforcement learning. Experiments on SWE-Bench Verified demonstrate improved interaction efficiency and action quality, while also highlighting challenges in aligning intermediate rewards with final task success.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SWE-Shepherd, a framework that employs Process Reward Models (PRMs) for step-level supervision to tackle inefficiencies and errors in automating real-world software engineering tasks using large language models (LLMs).
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for LLM-based automation in software engineering face challenges in handling long-term reasoning, consistent decision-making, and providing fine-grained feedback, leading to inefficient exploration and error propagation.
AI.method: The framework incorporates Process Reward Models (PRMs) trained on an action-level reward dataset using SWE-Bench trajectories to evaluate and guide intermediate actions. It employs a lightweight reward model to influence decisions without relying entirely on reinforcement learning.
AI.result: Experiments conducted on SWE-Bench Verified show improved interaction efficiency and action quality but also reveal challenges in aligning intermediate rewards with overall task success.
AI.conclusion: SWE-Shepherd demonstrates a promising step toward addressing inefficiencies and improving decision-making in LLM-based software engineering automation, though challenges in aligning rewards with task success remain.

title: Device-Conditioned Neural Architecture Search for Efficient Robotic Manipulation
summary: The growing complexity of visuomotor policies poses significant challenges for deployment with heterogeneous robotic hardware constraints. However, most existing model-efficient approaches for robotic manipulation are device- and model-specific, lack generalizability, and require time-consuming per-device optimization during the adaptation process. In this work, we propose a unified framework named \textbf{D}evice-\textbf{C}onditioned \textbf{Q}uantization-\textbf{F}or-\textbf{A}ll (DC-QFA) which amortizes deployment effort with the device-conditioned quantization-aware training and hardware-constrained architecture search. Specifically, we introduce a single supernet that spans a rich design space over network architectures and mixed-precision bit-widths. It is optimized with latency- and memory-aware regularization, guided by per-device lookup tables. With this supernet, for each target platform, we can perform a once-for-all lightweight search to select an optimal subnet without any per-device re-optimization, which enables more generalizable deployment across heterogeneous hardware, and substantially reduces deployment time. To improve long-horizon stability under low precision, we further introduce multi-step on-policy distillation to mitigate error accumulation during closed-loop execution. Extensive experiments on three representative policy backbones, such as DiffusionPolicy-T, MDT-V, and OpenVLA-OFT, demonstrate that our DC-QFA achieves $2\text{-}3\times$ acceleration on edge devices, consumer-grade GPUs, and cloud platforms, with negligible performance drop in task success. Real-world evaluations on an Inovo robot equipped with a force/torque sensor further validates that our low-bit DC-QFA policies maintain stable, contact-rich manipulation even under severe quantization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a unified framework named DC-QFA, designed to simplify the deployment of visuomotor policies across diverse robotic hardware. It uses device-conditioned quantization-aware training and hardware-constrained architecture search.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of deploying complex visuomotor policies across heterogeneous robotic hardware without needing device-specific optimizations.
AI.method: A single supernet optimized via latency- and memory-aware regularization, paired with multi-step on-policy distillation. This supernet enables lightweight hardware-specific searches without re-optimization.
AI.result: DC-QFA achieved 2-3x acceleration on edge devices, consumer-grade GPUs, and cloud platforms, with minimal performance loss in task success.
AI.conclusion: DC-QFA offers a generalizable solution for deploying visuomotor policies across varied hardware efficiently and reliably, maintaining performance even under severe quantization constraints.

title: CoSToM:Causal-oriented Steering for Intrinsic Theory-of-Mind Alignment in Large Language Models
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM), the ability to attribute mental states to others, is a hallmark of social intelligence. While large language models (LLMs) demonstrate promising performance on standard ToM benchmarks, we observe that they often fail to generalize to complex task-specific scenarios, relying heavily on prompt scaffolding to mimic reasoning. The critical misalignment between the internal knowledge and external behavior raises a fundamental question: Do LLMs truly possess intrinsic cognition, and can they externalize this internal knowledge into stable, high-quality behaviors? To answer this, we introduce CoSToM (Causal-oriented Steering for ToM alignment), a framework that transitions from mechanistic interpretation to active intervention. First, we employ causal tracing to map the internal distribution of ToM features, empirically uncovering the internal layers' characteristics in encoding fundamental ToM semantics. Building on this insight, we implement a lightweight alignment framework via targeted activation steering within these ToM-critical layers. Experiments demonstrate that CoSToM significantly enhances human-like social reasoning capabilities and downstream dialogue quality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoSToM, a framework designed to enhance Theory of Mind (ToM) capabilities in large language models (LLMs) by aligning their internal knowledge with external behavior.
AI.motivation: To address the misalignment between LLMs' internal cognition and their external behavior in Theory of Mind tasks, and to improve their generalization and reliability in complex ToM-specific scenarios.
AI.method: The proposed framework, CoSToM, uses causal tracing to map internal ToM feature distributions and applies targeted activation steering in critical layers to align internal knowledge with external behavior.
AI.result: CoSToM significantly improves LLMs' ability to perform human-like social reasoning and enhances the quality of ToM-relevant dialogues.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that aligning LLMs' internal mechanisms with their external behaviors can enhance their ToM performance, enabling more robust and human-like reasoning.

title: MobiFlow: Real-World Mobile Agent Benchmarking through Trajectory Fusion
summary: Mobile agents can autonomously complete user-assigned tasks through GUI interactions. However, existing mainstream evaluation benchmarks, such as AndroidWorld, operate by connecting to a system-level Android emulator and provide evaluation signals based on the state of system resources. In real-world mobile-agent scenarios, however, many third-party applications do not expose system-level APIs to determine whether a task has succeeded, leading to a mismatch between benchmarks and real-world usage and making it difficult to evaluate model performance accurately. To address these issues, we propose MobiFlow, an evaluation framework built on tasks drawn from arbitrary third-party applications. Using an efficient graph-construction algorithm based on multi-trajectory fusion, MobiFlow can effectively compress the state space, support dynamic interaction, and better align with real-world third-party application scenarios. MobiFlow covers 20 widely used third-party applications and comprises 240 diverse real-world tasks, with enriched evaluation metrics. Compared with AndroidWorld, MobiFlow's evaluation results show higher alignment with human assessments and can guide the training of future GUI-based models under real workloads.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MobiFlow, a new evaluation framework addressing flaws in existing benchmarks for real-world mobile-agent tasks by utilizing 20 third-party applications and 240 real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks like AndroidWorld fail to reflect real-world mobile-agent scenarios because they rely on system-level APIs unavailable in many third-party applications.
AI.method: MobiFlow employs a graph-construction algorithm based on multi-trajectory fusion to compress the state space and support dynamic interactions, making it suitable for third-party applications.
AI.result: The framework supports 20 popular third-party apps and 240 real-world tasks, providing enriched evaluation metrics that align better with human assessments compared to AndroidWorld.
AI.conclusion: MobiFlow enables better evaluation and guidance for training GUI-based mobile models in realistic scenarios, bridging the gap between benchmarks and real-world performance.

title: TaFall: Balance-Informed Fall Detection via Passive Thermal Sensing
summary: Falls are a major cause of injury and mortality among older adults, yet most incidents occur in private indoor environments where monitoring must balance effectiveness with privacy. Existing privacy-preserving fall detection approaches, particularly those based on radio frequency sensing, often rely on coarse motion cues, which limits reliability in real-world deployments. We introduce TaFall, a balance-informed fall detection system based on low-cost, privacy-preserving thermal array sensing. The key insight is that TaFall models a fall as a process of balance degradation and detects falls by estimating pose-driven biomechanical balance dynamics. To enable this capability from low-resolution thermal array maps, we propose (i) an appearance-motion fusion model for robust pose reconstruction, (ii) physically grounded balance-aware learning, and (iii) pose-bridged pretraining to improve robustness. TaFall achieves a detection rate of 98.26% with a false alarm rate of 0.65% on our dataset with over 3,000 fall instances from 35 participants across diverse indoor environments. In 27 day deployments across four homes, TaFall attains an ultra-low false alarm rate of 0.00126% and a pilot bathroom study confirms robustness under moisture and thermal interference. Together, these results establish TaFall as a reliable and privacy-preserving approach to fall detection in everyday living environments.

AI.tldr: TaFall is a privacy-preserving fall detection system using low-cost thermal array sensing, achieving high reliability in detecting falls with minimal false alarms, including in real-world environments.
AI.motivation: Falls are a leading cause of injury and mortality among older adults, especially in private indoor settings where effective monitoring must respect privacy.
AI.method: The system uses low-resolution thermal array sensing combined with biomechanical balance modeling, featuring an appearance-motion fusion model, balance-aware learning, and pose-bridged pretraining.
AI.result: TaFall achieved a 98.26% detection rate with a false alarm rate of 0.65% in lab tests and an ultra-low false alarm rate of 0.00126% in real-world deployments.
AI.conclusion: TaFall is a robust, privacy-focused fall detection system suitable for everyday indoor environments, demonstrating reliability under various conditions.

title: NeuroFlow: Toward Unified Visual Encoding and Decoding from Neural Activity
summary: Visual encoding and decoding models act as gateways to understanding the neural mechanisms underlying human visual perception. Typically, visual encoding models that predict brain activity from stimuli and decoding models that reproduce stimuli from brain activity are treated as distinct tasks, requiring separate models and training procedures. This separation is inefficient and fails to model the consistency between encoding and decoding processes. To address this limitation, we propose NeuroFlow, the first unified framework that jointly models visual encoding and decoding from neural activity within a single flow model. NeuroFlow introduces two key components: (1) NeuroVAE is designed as a variational backbone to model neural variability and establish a compact, semantically structured latent space for bidirectional modeling across visual and neural modalities. (2) Cross-modal Flow Matching (XFM) bypasses the typical paradigm of noise-to-data diffusion guided by a specific modality condition, instead learning a reversibly consistent flow model between visual and neural latent distributions. For the first time, visual encoding and decoding are reformulated as a time-dependent, reversible process within a shared latent space for unified modeling. Empirical results demonstrate that NeuroFlow achieves superior overall performance in visual encoding and decoding tasks with higher computational efficiency compared to any isolated methods. We further analyze principal factors that steer the model toward encoding-decoding consistency and, through brain functional analyses, demonstrate that NeuroFlow captures consistent activation patterns underlying neural variability. NeuroFlow marks a major step toward unified visual encoding and decoding from neural activity, providing mechanistic insights that inform future bidirectional visual brain-computer interfaces.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces NeuroFlow, a unified model for both visual encoding and decoding tasks using neural activity, offering improved performance and efficiency with a novel flow-based approach.
AI.motivation: Visual encoding and decoding are typically handled separately, leading to inefficiencies and lack of consistency between the two processes. This paper seeks to overcome these issues by creating a unified framework that integrates both tasks.
AI.method: The authors developed NeuroFlow, which includes two main components: (1) NeuroVAE, a variational backbone for creating a structured latent space for both visual and neural modalities, and (2) Cross-modal Flow Matching (XFM), a reversible flow model linking visual and neural latent distributions.
AI.result: NeuroFlow improves overall performance in encoding and decoding tasks while being computationally efficient. It also captures consistent neural activation patterns and ensures encoding-decoding consistency.
AI.conclusion: NeuroFlow provides a significant advancement in unifying visual encoding and decoding, paving the way for better visual brain-computer interfaces and deeper insights into neural mechanisms.

title: Universal statistical signatures of evolution in artificial intelligence architectures
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 4 supplementary tables. Code and data: https://github.com/mool32/ai-evolution-universal-signatures
summary: We test whether artificial intelligence architectural evolution obeys the same statistical laws as biological evolution. Compiling 935 ablation experiments from 161 publications, we show that the distribution of fitness effects (DFE) of architectural modifications follows a heavy-tailed Student's t-distribution with proportions (68% deleterious, 19% neutral, 13% beneficial for major ablations, n=568) that place AI between compact viral genomes and simple eukaryotes. The DFE shape matches D. melanogaster (normalized KS=0.07) and S. cerevisiae (KS=0.09); the elevated beneficial fraction (13% vs. 1-6% in biology) quantifies the advantage of directed over blind search while preserving the distributional form. Architectural origination follows logistic dynamics (R^2=0.994) with punctuated equilibria and adaptive radiation into domain niches. Fourteen architectural traits were independently invented 3-5 times, paralleling biological convergences. These results demonstrate that the statistical structure of evolution is substrate-independent, determined by fitness landscape topology rather than the mechanism of selection.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the statistical laws governing artificial intelligence (AI) architectural evolution by analyzing 935 ablation experiments from 161 publications, revealing similarities with biological evolution but highlighting key differences.
AI.motivation: To explore whether the principles of statistical evolution apply universally across biological and artificial systems, and determine if AI evolution adheres to biological patterns.
AI.method: The study utilizes a compilation of 935 ablation experiments to characterize the fitness effects distribution (DFE), matches distributions to biological models, examines the dynamics of architectural origination, and compares AI traits to biological convergence.
AI.result: Finds that AI evolution shares a statistical structure with biological evolution, including heavy-tailed fitness effects distributions akin to genomes of organisms, logistic origination dynamics, punctuated equilibria, adaptive radiation, and convergent invention of architectural traits.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary principles are substrate-independent, and the statistical structure of architectural modification in AI systems aligns with biological evolution, shaped by fitness landscape topology rather than selection mechanisms.

title: ECHO: Elastic Speculative Decoding with Sparse Gating for High-Concurrency Scenarios
summary: Speculative Decoding promises to accelerate the inference of Large Language Models, yet its efficacy often degrades in production-grade serving. Existing evaluations typically overlook the compute-bound nature of high-concurrency regimes, where verification compute becomes the dominant bottleneck. Consequently, prior methods face a dilemma: static trees incur massive verification waste, while dynamic trees suffer from cumulative misjudgments and kernel incompatibility. To bridge this gap, we introduce ECHO, a high concurrency-oriented framework integrated into SGLang that reformulates speculative execution as a budgeted scheduling problem. Crucially, ECHO employs sparse confidence gating to manage the batch as a unified super-tree, elastically pivoting budget between depth and width to co-optimize the trade-off between reducing global verification steps and maximizing per-step efficiency. Extensive evaluations across diverse model scales-particularly the industrial-grade Qwen3-235B-demonstrate that ECHO consistently outperforms SOTA methods in both low-load and high-load scenarios, achieving up to 5.35x walltime speedup and delivering over 20% relative speedup gain.

AI.tldr: ECHO is introduced as a framework that enhances speculative decoding performance for Large Language Models in high-concurrency scenarios. It focuses on balancing verification efficiency and reducing computational bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: Prior speculative decoding methods degrade in efficacy under compute-heavy, high-concurrency production serving. Static and dynamic tree methods are inefficient due to either verification waste or errors.
AI.method: ECHO redefines speculative execution as a budgeted scheduling problem, using sparse confidence gating to treat computational tasks as a unified super-tree. This approach adjusts resources between depth and width dynamically to optimize verification and efficiency.
AI.result: ECHO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving up to 5.35x walltime speedup and over 20% relative speedup across various models, including the industrial-grade Qwen3-235B.
AI.conclusion: ECHO demonstrates significant and reliable performance improvements in speculative decoding, especially in high-load, compute-bound environments, making it practical for industrial-scale AI applications.

title: CapBench: A Multi-PDK Dataset for Machine-Learning-Based Post-Layout Capacitance Extraction
comment: Accepted at the 63rd ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC '26). 7 pages, 5 figures
summary: We present CapBench, a fully reproducible, multi-PDK dataset for capacitance extraction. The dataset is derived from open-source designs, including single-core CPUs, systems-on-chip, and media accelerators. All designs are fully placed and routed using 14 independent OpenROAD flow runs spanning three technology nodes: ASAP7, NanGate45, and Sky130HD. From these layouts, we extract 61,855 3D windows across three size tiers to enable transfer learning and scalability studies. High-fidelity capacitance labels are generated using RWCap, a state-of-the-art random-walk solver, and validated against the industry-standard Raphael, achieving a mean absolute error of 0.64% for total capacitance. Each window is pre-processed into density maps, graph representations, and point clouds. We evaluate 10 machine learning architectures that illustrate dataset usage and serve as baselines, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs), point cloud transformers, and graph neural networks (GNNs). CNNs demonstrate the lowest errors (1.75%), while GNNs are up to 41.4x faster but exhibit larger errors (10.2%), illustrating a clear accuracy-speed trade-off. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/THU-numbda/CapBench.

AI.tldr: CapBench introduces a reproducible, open-source dataset for capacitance extraction, derived from various CPU and SoC designs across three technology nodes, and evaluates ML architectures for accuracy and speed trade-offs.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the need for reproducible and scalable datasets in capacitance extraction using open-source methodologies to enhance transfer learning and machine learning studies.
AI.method: CapBench involves the creation of a multi-PDK dataset from designs routed across three nodes using OpenROAD and extracts 61,855 3D windows for testing multiple ML architectures.
AI.result: CapBench achieves high fidelity in capacitance labels validated against industry standards with CNNs showing best accuracy (1.75%) but slower performance, while GNNs exhibit faster computation with increased error (10.2%).
AI.conclusion: CapBench serves as a benchmark for capacitance extraction studies with clear accuracy-speed trade-offs among evaluated machine learning architectures, offering a valuable tool for academic and industrial research.

title: Agentic Compilation: Mitigating the LLM Rerun Crisis for Minimized-Inference-Cost Web Automation
summary: LLM-driven web agents operating through continuous inference loops -- repeatedly querying a model to evaluate browser state and select actions -- exhibit a fundamental scalability constraint for repetitive tasks. We characterize this as the Rerun Crisis: the linear growth of token expenditure and API latency relative to execution frequency. For a 5-step workflow over 500 iterations, a continuous agent incurs approximately 150.00 USD in inference costs; even with aggressive caching, this remains near 15.00 USD. We propose a Compile-and-Execute architecture that decouples LLM reasoning from browser execution, reducing per-workflow inference cost to under 0.10 USD. A one-shot LLM invocation processes a token-efficient semantic representation from a DOM Sanitization Module (DSM) and emits a deterministic JSON workflow blueprint. A lightweight runtime then drives the browser without further model queries. We formalize this cost reduction from O(M x N) to amortized O(1) inference scaling, where M is the number of reruns and N is the sequential actions. Empirical evaluation across data extraction, form filling, and fingerprinting tasks yields zero-shot compilation success rates of 80-94%. Crucially, the modularity of the JSON intermediate representation allows minimal Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) patching to elevate execution reliability to near-100%. At per-compilation costs between 0.002 USD and 0.092 USD across five frontier models, these results establish deterministic compilation as a paradigm enabling economically viable automation at scales previously infeasible under continuous architectures.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the high costs of LLM-based web agents for repetitive tasks due to continuous inference loops and introduces a new Compile-and-Execute architecture to reduce costs drastically.
AI.motivation: To overcome the scalability limitations and high token/API expenses faced by current LLM-driven web agents during repetitive web automation tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose a Compile-and-Execute architecture where a single LLM invocation creates a semantic JSON blueprint via a DOM Sanitization Module, followed by execution using a lightweight runtime without further model queries.
AI.result: The solution minimizes costs from O(M x N) to amortized O(1), achieving compilation success rates of 80-94%, near-100% execution reliability with minimal HITL intervention, and per-compilation costs ranging from 0.002 USD to 0.092 USD.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic compilation enables scalable and cost-effective LLM-driven automation, addressing previous economic infeasibilities in continuous architectures for repetitive tasks.

title: Performance of weakly-supervised electronic health record-based phenotyping methods in rare-outcome settings
comment: 58 pages, 4 main figures, 3 supplemental figures, 4 main tables, 17 supplemental tables
summary: Accurately identifying patients with specific medical conditions is a key challenge when using clinical data from electronic health records. Our objective was to comprehensively assess when weakly-supervised prediction methods, which use silver-standard labels (proxy measures of the true outcome) rather than gold-standard true labels, perform well in rare-outcome settings like vaccine safety studies. We compared three methods (PheNorm, MAP, and sureLDA) that combine structured features and features derived from clinical text using natural language processing, through an extensive simulation study with data-generating mechanisms ranging from simple to complex, varying outcome rates, and varying degrees of informative silver labels. We also considered using predicted probabilities to design a chart review validation study. No single method dominated the other across all prediction performance metrics. Probability-guided sampling selected a cohort enriched for patients with more mentions of important concepts in chart notes. SureLDA, the most complex of the three algorithms we considered, often performed well in simulations. Performance depended greatly on selected tuning parameters. Care should be taken when using weakly-supervised prediction methods in rare-outcome settings, particularly if the probabilities will be used in downstream analysis, but these methods can work well when silver labels are strong predictors of true outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates weakly-supervised prediction methods using silver-standard labels for identifying medical conditions in rare-outcome settings. Simulations analyze performance of three algorithms.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of identifying patients with medical conditions accurately using weakly-supervised methods and silver-standard labels, especially in rare-outcome scenarios like vaccine safety studies.
AI.method: Three algorithms (PheNorm, MAP, sureLDA) were compared through extensive simulations that varied outcome rates, data complexities, and silver-label informativeness. Probability-guided sampling was also evaluated.
AI.result: SureLDA often performed well but required careful tuning; no method universally outperformed others. Probability-guided sampling enriched cohorts for mentions of key concepts in clinical text.
AI.conclusion: Weakly-supervised methods can work effectively with informative silver labels but require caution in rare-outcome settings, especially for downstream probability use.

title: How Many Tries Does It Take? Iterative Self-Repair in LLM Code Generation Across Model Scales and Benchmarks
summary: Large language models frequently fail to produce correct code on their first attempt, yet most benchmarks evaluate them in a single-shot setting. We investigate iterative self-repair (feeding execution errors back to the model for correction) across seven models spanning three families and both open-weight and proprietary providers: Llama 3.1 8B, Llama 3.3 70B, Llama 4 Scout (MoE, 16 experts), Llama 4 Maverick (MoE, 128 experts), Qwen3 32B, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 2.5 Pro. On HumanEval (164 problems) and MBPP Sanitized (257 problems) with up to five attempts, self-repair universally improves pass rates: +4.9 to +17.1 pp on HumanEval and +16.0 to +30.0 pp on MBPP. Gemini 2.5 Flash achieves the highest final pass rates (96.3% HumanEval, 93.8% MBPP). Most gains concentrate in the first two rounds.Error-type analysis shows assertion errors (logical mistakes) are the hardest to repair at ~45%, while syntax and name errors are repaired at substantially higher rates, connecting to broader findings on the limits of LLM self-correction. Prior work found that weaker models fail at self-repair or require fine-tuning; we show that modern instruction-tuned models succeed with prompting alone, even at 8B scale. We also provide the first comparison of dense and MoE architectures for self-repair, and extend the repair-vs-resampling tradeoff analysis to modern models. A prompt ablation reveals chain-of-thought repair yields up to +5.5 pp additional self-repair gain (measured as improvement in repair delta) over minimal prompting for capable models.

AI.tldr: The paper explores iterative self-repair in large language models by feeding execution errors back for correction, showing consistent improvement in code pass rates across various models.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of single-shot evaluation in large language models, emphasizing iterative self-repair as a learning paradigm.
AI.method: Evaluated iterative self-repair performance across seven language models using benchmarks such as HumanEval and MBPP Sanitized. Also analyzed error types and architecture differences.
AI.result: Self-repair increased pass rates universally, with the most significant improvements in the first two rounds and Gemini 2.5 Flash achieving the highest pass rates. Assertion errors proved hardest to fix.
AI.conclusion: Modern instruction-tuned models are capable of effective self-repair through prompting alone, even at lower scales, and chain-of-thought repair enhances correction capability further.

title: ReaLiTy and LADS: A Unified Framework and Dataset Suite for LiDAR Adaptation Across Sensors and Adverse Weather Conditions
summary: Reliable LiDAR perception requires robustness across sensors, environments, and adverse weather. However, existing datasets rarely provide physically consistent observations of the same scene under varying sensor configurations and weather conditions, limiting systematic analysis of domain shifts. This work presents ReaLiTy, a unified physics-informed framework that transforms LiDAR data to match target sensor specifications and weather conditions. The framework integrates physically grounded cues with a learning-based module to generate realistic intensity patterns, while a physics-based weather model introduces consistent geometric and radiometric degradations. Building on this framework, we introduce the LiDAR Adaptation Dataset Suite (LADS), a collection of physically consistent, transformation-ready point clouds with one-to-one correspondence to original datasets. Experiments demonstrate improved cross-domain consistency and realistic weather effects. ReaLiTy and LADS provide a reproducible foundation for studying LiDAR adaptation and simulation-driven perception in intelligent transportation systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ReaLiTy, a framework for transforming LiDAR data across varying sensor setups and weather conditions, along with a new LiDAR dataset suite for domain adaptation research.
AI.motivation: The motivation is that current LiDAR datasets lack physically consistent observations under diverse sensor and weather scenarios, which limits the analysis of domain shifts for robust perception.
AI.method: The proposed ReaLiTy framework combines physics-based models and a learning-based module to transform LiDAR data realistically while simulating geometric and radiometric effects of adverse weather.
AI.result: Experiments show that ReaLiTy improves cross-domain consistency and simulates realistic weather effects for LiDAR data transformations.
AI.conclusion: ReaLiTy, coupled with the LADS dataset suite, serves as a robust framework for studying LiDAR adaptation and advancing simulation-driven perception for intelligent transportation systems.

title: Computational Implementation of a Model of Category-Theoretic Metaphor Comprehension
summary: In this study, we developed a computational implementation for a model of metaphor comprehension based on the theory of indeterminate natural transformation (TINT) proposed by Fuyama et al. We simplified the algorithms implementing the model to be closer to the original theory and verified it through data fitting and simulations. The outputs of the algorithms are evaluated with three measures: data-fitting with experimental data, the systematicity of the metaphor comprehension result, and the novelty of the comprehension (i.e. the correspondence of the associative structure of the source and target of the metaphor). The improved algorithm outperformed the existing ones in all the three measures.

AI.tldr: This paper develops and evaluates a computational model of metaphor comprehension based on the TINT theory.
AI.motivation: To refine and validate a computational algorithm for metaphor comprehension by aligning it more closely with the TINT theory and improving its performance.
AI.method: The researchers simplified the computational algorithms based on TINT theory and evaluated their performance using three measures: data-fitting with experimental data, systematic comprehension, and novelty in associative structure.
AI.result: The improved algorithm performed better than existing models in data-fitting, systematicity, and novelty of metaphor comprehension.
AI.conclusion: A simplified and improved TINT-based algorithm effectively models metaphor comprehension and achieves superior results across multiple evaluation criteria.

title: Persistent Identity in AI Agents: A Multi-Anchor Architecture for Resilient Memory and Continuity
comment: 18 pages, 2 figures. Submitting to arXiv cs.ET (Emerging Technologies)
summary: Modern AI agents suffer from a fundamental identity problem: when context windows overflow and conversation histories are summarized, agents experience catastrophic forgetting -- losing not just information, but continuity of self. This technical limitation reflects a deeper architectural flaw: AI agent identity is centralized in a single memory store, creating a single point of failure. Drawing on neurological case studies of human memory disorders, we observe that human identity survives damage because it is distributed across multiple systems: episodic memory, procedural memory, emotional continuity, and embodied knowledge. We present soul.py, an open-source architecture that implements persistent identity through separable components (identity files and memory logs), and propose extensions toward multi-anchor resilience. The framework introduces a hybrid RAG+RLM retrieval system that automatically routes queries to appropriate memory access patterns, achieving efficient retrieval without sacrificing comprehensiveness. We formalize the notion of identity anchors for AI systems and present a roadmap for building agents whose identity can survive partial memory failures. Code is available at github.com/menonpg/soul.py

AI.tldr: This paper introduces soul.py, a framework addressing AI identity issues by utilizing distributed memory akin to human systems, ensuring resilience against partial memory failures.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the issue of catastrophic forgetting and the centralized memory architecture of modern AI agents, which leads to identity loss during memory overflow and summarization.
AI.method: The paper introduces soul.py, an open-source architecture that distributes identity across components like identity files and memory logs, using a hybrid RAG+RLM retrieval system for efficient and comprehensive memory access.
AI.result: Soul.py achieves efficient memory retrieval while maintaining comprehensive access patterns. It provides a formalization of identity anchors and a roadmap for resilient AI identity.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a new approach to AI agent identity, emphasizing distributed components inspired by human memory systems and presenting strategies to prevent identity failure from partial memory loss.

title: EDFNet: Early Fusion of Edge and Depth for Thin-Obstacle Segmentation in UAV Navigation
summary: Autonomous Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) must reliably detect thin obstacles such as wires, poles, and branches to navigate safely in real-world environments. These structures remain difficult to perceive because they occupy few pixels, often exhibit weak visual contrast, and are strongly affected by class imbalance. Existing segmentation methods primarily target coarser obstacles and do not fully exploit the complementary multimodal cues needed for thin-structure perception. We present EDFNet, a modular early-fusion segmentation framework that integrates RGB, depth, and edge information for thin-obstacle perception in cluttered aerial scenes. We evaluate EDFNet on the Drone Depth and Obstacle Segmentation (DDOS) dataset across sixteen modality-backbone configurations using U-Net and DeepLabV3 in pretrained and non-pretrained settings. The results show that early RGB-Depth-Edge fusion provides a competitive and well-balanced baseline, with the most consistent gains appearing in boundary-sensitive and recall-oriented metrics. The pretrained RGBDE U-Net achieves the best overall performance, with the highest Thin-Structure Evaluation Score (0.244), mean IoU (0.219), and boundary IoU (0.234), while maintaining competitive runtime performance (19.62 FPS) on our evaluation hardware. However, performance on the rarest ultra-thin categories remains low across all models, indicating that reliable ultra-thin segmentation is still an open challenge. Overall, these findings position early RGB-Depth-Edge fusion as a practical and modular baseline for thin-obstacle segmentation in UAV navigation.

AI.tldr: EDFNet integrates RGB, depth, and edge information to improve UAV obstacle detection, achieving better thin-structure segmentation in aerial scenes.
AI.motivation: Improve UAV navigation by addressing challenges in detecting thin obstacles, which are difficult to perceive and affected by class imbalance.
AI.method: Developed EDFNet, an early fusion framework combining RGB, depth, and edge modalities; evaluated it with various configurations on the DDOS dataset.
AI.result: EDFNet achieves competitive segmentation results, with pretrained RGBDE U-Net showing the highest evaluation scores and runtime performance. Ultra-thin categories remain challenging.
AI.conclusion: Early RGB-Depth-Edge fusion provides a practical baseline for UAV navigation, although ultra-thin obstacle detection needs further research.

title: Below-ground Fungal Biodiversity Can be Monitored Using Self-Supervised Learning Satellite Features
summary: Mycorrhizal fungi are vital to terrestrial ecosystem functioning. Yet monitoring their biodiversity at landscape scales is often unfeasible due to time and cost constraints. Current predictions suggest that 90\% of mycorrhizal diversity hotspots remain unprotected, opening questions of how to broadly and effectively map underground fungal communities. Here, we show that self-supervised learning (SSL) applied to satellite imagery can predict below-ground ectomycorrhizal fungal richness across diverse environments. Our models explain over half the variance in species richness across ~12,000 field samples spanning Europe and Asia. SSL-derived features prove to be the single most informative predictor, subsuming the majority of information contained in climate, soil, and land cover datasets. Using this approach, we achieve a 10,000-fold increase in spatial resolution over existing techniques, moving from 1km landscape averages to 10m habitat-scale observations with nearly no systematic bias. As satellite observations are dynamic rather than static, this enables temporal monitoring of below-ground biodiversity at landscape scales for the first time. We analyze multi-year trends in predicted fungal richness across UK National Park woodlands, finding that ancient forests may be losing ectomycorrhizal diversity at disproportionate rates. These results establish SSL satellite features as a scalable tool for extending sparse field observations to continuous, high-resolution biodiversity maps for monitoring the invisible half of terrestrial ecosystems.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates the use of self-supervised learning (SSL) on satellite imagery to map and monitor ectomycorrhizal fungal biodiversity at high resolution across landscapes.
AI.motivation: Most mycorrhizal diversity hotspots remain unprotected, and effectively mapping their communities is infeasible due to cost and time constraints. There is a need for scalable methods to monitor these essential underground fungal ecosystems.
AI.method: The study applies SSL to satellite imagery to predict ectomycorrhizal fungal richness. Models built using SSL features were tested against ~12,000 field samples across Europe and Asia, and compared with traditional predictors like climate and soil data.
AI.result: The SSL models explained over 50% of the species richness variance across the field samples, offered a 10,000-fold increase in spatial resolution, and enabled temporal biodiversity monitoring. Multi-year trends indicated disproportionate diversity loss in ancient forests.
AI.conclusion: SSL-derived satellite features are effective, scalable tools for creating continuous biodiversity maps, enabling high-resolution and temporal monitoring of below-ground fungal ecosystems.

title: INCRT: An Incremental Transformer That Determines Its Own Architecture
comment: 19 pages, 6 figures, 5 theorems. Submitted to Neurocomputing (Elsevier)
summary:
Transformer architectures are designed by trial and error: the number of attention heads, the depth, and the head size are fixed before training begins, with no mathematical principle to guide the choice. The result is systematic structural redundancy -- between half and four-fifths of all heads in a trained model can be removed without measurable loss -- because the architecture allocates capacity without reference to the actual requirements of the task.This paper introduces INCRT (Incremental Transformer), an architecture that determines its own structure during training. Starting from a single head, INCRT adds one attention head at a time whenever its current configuration is provably insufficient, and prunes heads that have become redundant. Each growth decision is driven by a single, online-computable geometric quantity derived from the task's directional structure, requiring no separate validation phase and no hand-tuned schedule.
  Two theorems form the theoretical backbone. The first (homeostatic convergence) establishes that the system always reaches a finite stopping configuration that is simultaneously minimal (no redundant heads) and sufficient (no uncaptured directional energy above the threshold). The second (compressed-sensing analogy) provides a geometric upper bound on the number of heads that this configuration can contain, as a function of the spectral complexity of the task.
  Experiments on SARS-CoV-2 variant classification and SST-2 sentiment analysis confirm both results: the predicted and observed head counts agree within 12% across all benchmarks, and the final architectures match or exceed BERT-base on distribution-specific tasks while using between three and seven times fewer parameters and no pre-training.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces INCRT, an adaptable Transformer model that optimizes its architecture during training by adding or removing attention heads as needed, significantly reducing redundancy and computational requirements.
AI.motivation: Transformer architectures often have structural redundancy because they are designed without task-specific guidance, leading to inefficiencies. The paper aims to address this by creating a method to adaptively design Transformers based on task requirements.
AI.method: The INCRT architecture incrementally adjusts its structure during training by adding or pruning attention heads based on a geometric quantity derived from the task. The process is theoretically supported by two theorems ensuring sufficiency and minimization.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrated that INCRT achieves comparable or better performance than BERT-base on specific tasks (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 classification and sentiment analysis) while using 3-7 times fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: INCRT proves that Transformer models can dynamically self-optimize their structure to meet task requirements, providing both efficiency and strong performance without pre-training.

title: LLM-assisted Agentic Edge Intelligence Framework
summary: Edge intelligence delivers low-latency inference, yet most edge analytics remain hard-coded and must be redeployed as conditions change. When data patterns shift or new questions arise, engineers often need to write new scripts and push updates to devices, which slows iteration and raises operating costs. This limited adaptability reduces scalability and autonomy in large, heterogeneous, and resource-constrained edge deployments, and it increases reliance on human oversight. Meanwhile, large language models (LLMs) can interpret instructions and generate code, but their compute and memory requirements typically prevent direct deployment on edge devices. We address this gap with the LLM-assisted Edge Intelligence (LEI) framework, which removes the need for manually specified business logic. In LEI, a cloud-hosted LLM coordinates the creation and update of device-side logic as requirements evolve. The system generates candidate lightweight programs, checks them against available data and constraints, and then deploys the selected version to each device. This lets each device receive a tailored program based on sample data, metadata, context, and current resource limits. We evaluate LEI on four heterogeneous datasets, including air quality, temperature \& humidity, wind, and soil datasets using multiple LLM backends. The experimental results show that the framework maintains low average CPU and memory utilization during the execution. These results indicate that the framework adapts efficiently to changing conditions while maintaining resource efficiency.

AI.tldr: LEI framework integrates cloud-hosted LLMs to generate tailored logic for edge devices, addressing scalability and adaptability challenges.
AI.motivation: To overcome hard-coded analytics and improve scalability and autonomy in edge intelligence.
AI.method: Combines LLMs hosted on the cloud to generate and deploy custom programs suited to edge device conditions.
AI.result: Achieves efficient adaptation to changing data patterns with low CPU and memory usage.
AI.conclusion: LEI enhances adaptability of edge devices while keeping resource utilization low, facilitating dynamic and scalable deployments.

title: EdgeCIM: A Hardware-Software Co-Design for CIM-Based Acceleration of Small Language Models
summary: The growing demand for deploying Small Language Models (SLMs) on edge devices, including laptops, smartphones, and embedded platforms, has exposed fundamental inefficiencies in existing accelerators. While GPUs handle prefill workloads efficiently, the autoregressive decoding phase is dominated by GEMV operations that are inherently memory-bound, resulting in poor utilization and prohibitive energy costs at the edge. In this work, we present EdgeCIM, a hardware-software co-design framework that rethinks accelerator design for end-to-end decoder-only inference.
