To address this, we propose CircuitSynth, a novel neuro-symbolic framework that decouples semantic reasoning from surface realization. By distilling the reasoning capabilities of a Teacher LLM into a Probabilistic Sentential Decision Diagram (PSDD), CircuitSynth creates a tractable semantic prior that structurally enforces hard logical constraints. Furthermore, we introduce a convex optimization mechanism to rigorously satisfy soft distributional goals. Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that CircuitSynth achieves 100% Schema Validity even in complex logic puzzles where unconstrained baselines fail (12.4%) while significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods in rare-combination coverage.

AI.tldr: CircuitSynth addresses hallucinations and inconsistencies in LLM-driven data generation by separating semantic reasoning and surface realization. It uses PSDD for logical constraints and convex optimization for distributional goals, achieving superior validity and coverage.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs struggle with consistency, validity, and balanced linguistic expression in structured generation tasks.
AI.method: CircuitSynth employs a neuro-symbolic framework, using a Teacher LLM to distill reasoning into a Probabilistic Sentential Decision Diagram (PSDD), coupled with convex optimization for soft distributional goals.
AI.result: Experiments show CircuitSynth achieves 100% schema validity in tasks where alternatives fail significantly and excels in rare-combination coverage.
AI.conclusion: CircuitSynth offers a robust, logically constrained framework that ensures high-fidelity synthetic data generation, outperforming current techniques.

title: LLMs for Text-Based Exploration and Navigation Under Partial Observability
comment: 15 pages, (to be published Springer Lecture Notes of the Institute for Computer Sciences, Social Informatics and Telecommunications Engineering [LNICST] )
summary: Exploration and goal-directed navigation in unknown layouts are central to inspection, logistics, and search-and-rescue. We ask whether large language models (LLMs) can function as \emph{text-only} controllers under partial observability -- without code execution, tools, or program synthesis. We introduce a reproducible benchmark with oracle localisation in fixed ASCII gridworlds: each step reveals only a local $5\times5$ window around the agent and the model must select one of \texttt{UP/RIGHT/DOWN/LEFT}. Nine contemporary LLMs ranging from open/proprietary, dense / Mixture of Experts and instruction- vs. reasoning-tuned are evaluated on two tasks across three layouts of increasing difficulty: \emph{Exploration} (maximising revealed cells) and \emph{Navigation} (reach the goal on the shortest path). The experimental results are evaluated on quantitative metrics including \emph{success rate}, \emph{efficiency} such as normalised coverage and \emph{path length} vs. oracle as well as qualitative analysis. Reasoning-tuned models reliably complete navigation across all layouts, yet remain less efficient than oracle paths. Few-shot demonstrations in the prompt chiefly help these Reasoning-tuned models by reducing invalid moves and shortening paths, while classic dense instruction models remain inconsistent. We observe characteristic action priors (UP/RIGHT) that can induce looping under partial observability. Overall, training regimen and test-time deliberation predict control ability better than raw parameter count. These findings suggest lightweight hybridisation with classical online planners as a practical route to deployable partial map systems.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether large language models (LLMs) can serve as text-only controllers for navigation tasks in partially visible gridworlds, evaluating multiple LLMs on exploration and goal-directed tasks.
AI.motivation: The study aims to explore if LLMs can perform navigation in unknown environments using text-based inputs, addressing needs in scenarios like inspection and search-and-rescue without reliance on code or tools.
AI.method: Using a reproducible benchmark, the authors evaluate nine LLMs on exploration and navigation tasks in ASCII gridworlds, analyzing their success rates, efficiency, and tendencies under partial observability.
AI.result: Reasoning-tuned LLMs performed better in navigation tasks but were less efficient compared to oracle paths, benefiting from few-shot demonstrations. Instruction models were less reliable.
AI.conclusion: LLM performance is influenced more by training and decision-making strategies than by model size, suggesting a hybrid approach with traditional online planners for practical applications.

title: The Deployment Gap in AI Media Detection: Platform-Aware and Visually Constrained Adversarial Evaluation
summary:
Recent AI media detectors report near-perfect performance under clean laboratory evaluation, yet their robustness under realistic deployment conditions remains underexplored. In practice, AI-generated images are resized, compressed, re-encoded, and visually modified before being shared on online platforms. We argue that this creates a deployment gap between laboratory robustness and real-world reliability.
  In this work, we introduce a platform-aware adversarial evaluation framework for AI media detection that explicitly models deployment transforms (e.g., resizing, compression, screenshot-style distortions) and constrains perturbations to visually plausible meme-style bands rather than full-image noise. Under this threat model, detectors achieving AUC $\approx$ 0{.}99 in clean settings experience substantial degradation. Per-image platform-aware attacks reduce AUC to significantly lower levels and achieve high fake-to-real misclassification rates, despite strict visual constraints. We further demonstrate that universal perturbations exist even under localized band constraints, revealing shared vulnerability directions across inputs. Beyond accuracy degradation, we observe pronounced calibration collapse under attack, where detectors become confidently incorrect.
  Our findings highlight that robustness measured under clean conditions substantially overestimates deployment robustness. We advocate for platform-aware evaluation as a necessary component of future AI media security benchmarks and release our evaluation framework to facilitate standardized robustness assessment.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new framework to evaluate AI media detectors' robustness to real-world transformations, finding a performance gap between laboratory and deployment settings.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the performance disparity between AI media detectors' laboratory evaluations and their real-world deployment conditions, focusing on how common media transformations impact detection reliability.
AI.method: The researchers developed a platform-aware adversarial evaluation framework that models deployment transformations like resizing and compression, and limits perturbations to visually plausible regions to simulate realistic challenges.
AI.result: The study demonstrates that detectors with high accuracy in clean settings perform poorly under realistic deployment conditions, facing accuracy degradation, increased misclassification rates, and calibration collapse under attacks.
AI.conclusion: AI media detectors' current evaluation methods overestimate real-world robustness. The authors recommend incorporating platform-aware evaluation into future AI media security benchmarks and provide tools for standardized testing.

title: A Tale of Two Temperatures: Simple, Efficient, and Diverse Sampling from Diffusion Language Models
summary: Much work has been done on designing fast and accurate sampling for diffusion language models (dLLMs). However, these efforts have largely focused on the tradeoff between speed and quality of individual samples; how to additionally ensure diversity across samples remains less well understood. In this work, we show that diversity can be increased by using softened, tempered versions of familiar confidence-based remasking heuristics, retaining their computational benefits and offering simple implementations. We motivate this approach by introducing an idealized formal model of fork tokens and studying the impact of remasking on the expected entropy at the forks. Empirically, the proposed tempered heuristics close the exploration gap (pass@k) between existing confidence-based and autoregressive sampling, hence outperforming both when controlling for cost (pass@NFE). We further study how the increase in diversity translates to downstream post-training and test-time compute scaling. Overall, our findings demonstrate that simple, efficient, and diverse sampling from dLLMs is possible.

AI.tldr: This paper focuses on improving sample diversity in diffusion language models (dLLMs) by using tempered confidence-based remasking heuristics, enhancing diversity while retaining efficiency and simplicity.
AI.motivation: Existing research on dLLMs has concentrated on optimizing the tradeoff between sample speed and quality, but little is understood about ensuring diversity across generated samples, an essential factor for many applications.
AI.method: The approach introduces a softened, tempered version of confidence-based remasking heuristics and develops an idealized model of fork tokens to analyze the expected entropy impact on diversity.
AI.result: The tempered heuristics method closes the exploration gap between existing confidence-based methods and autoregressive sampling, outperforming both in diversity and cost-efficiency metrics like pass@NFE.
AI.conclusion: Efficient, diverse, and simple sampling methodologies for dLLMs can be achieved using tempered confidence-based remasking, which benefits both computation and application scale.

title: Hotspot-Aware Scheduling of Virtual Machines with Overcommitment for Ultimate Utilization in Cloud Datacenters
summary: We address the problem of under-utilization of resources in datacenters during cloud operations, specifically focusing on the challenge of online virtual machine (VM) scheduling. Rather than following the traditional approach of scheduling VMs based solely on their static flavors, we take into account their dynamic CPU utilization. We employ $Γ$-robustness theory to manage the dynamic nature and introduce a novel variant of bin packing - Probabilistic k-Bins Packing (PkBP), which theoretically protects the Physical Machines (PMs) from hotspots formation within a specified probability $α$. We develop a scheduling algroithm named CloseRadiusFit and cold-start AI based prediction algorithms for the online version of PkBP. To verify the quality of our approach towards the optimal solutions, we solve the Offline PkBP problem by designing a novel Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) model and a combination of numerical upper and lower bounds. Our experimental results demonstrate that CloseRadiusFit achieves narrow gaps of 1.6% and 3.1% when compared to the lower and upper bounds, respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel approach for cloud datacenter resource utilization by addressing online virtual machine (VM) scheduling using probabilistic and AI-based methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve resource utilization in datacenters, which is often challenged by under-utilization and inefficient scheduling of VMs based on static parameters rather than dynamic CPU utilization.
AI.method: The authors propose a theoretical model (Probabilistic k-Bins Packing or PkBP) alongside a scheduling algorithm named CloseRadiusFit and AI-based prediction techniques. Additionally, they introduce a Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) model for offline optimization.
AI.result: Their experimental findings show that the CloseRadiusFit algorithm achieves minimal performance gaps of 1.6% and 3.1% compared to optimal lower and upper bounds, respectively.
AI.conclusion: This work innovates by considering dynamic CPU utilization for VM scheduling, achieving near-optimal performance and preventing PM hotspots through robust probabilistic modeling.

title: ShapShift: Explaining Model Prediction Shifts with Subgroup Conditional Shapley Values
summary: Changes in input distribution can induce shifts in the average predictions of machine learning models. Such prediction shifts may impact downstream business outcomes (e.g. a bank's loan approval rate), so understanding their causes can be crucial. We propose \ours{}: a Shapley value method for attributing prediction shifts to changes in the conditional probabilities of interpretable subgroups of data, where these subgroups are defined by the structure of decision trees. We initially apply this method to single decision trees, providing exact explanations based on conditional probability changes at split nodes. Next, we extend it to tree ensembles by selecting the most explanatory tree and accounting for residual effects. Finally, we propose a model-agnostic variant using surrogate trees grown with a novel objective function, allowing application to models like neural networks. While exact computation can be intensive, approximation techniques enable practical application. We show that \ours{} provides simple, faithful, and near-complete explanations of prediction shifts across model classes, aiding model monitoring in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces \ours{}, a Shapley value-based method to explain prediction shifts caused by changes in data distribution, applicable across various models including decision trees and neural networks.
AI.motivation: To understand and attribute the causes of prediction shifts in machine learning models, which can impact critical business outcomes.
AI.method: The paper proposes \ours{}, leveraging decision tree structures and Shapley values to explain prediction shifts based on conditional probabilities of data subgroups. Extensions include ensemble trees and model-agnostic variants using surrogate trees.
AI.result: \ours{} achieves reliable explanations for prediction shifts, applicable to decision trees, ensembles, and neural networks, with approximation techniques for scalability.
AI.conclusion: \ours{} enhances model monitoring by providing clear and effective explanations for prediction shifts, valuable in dynamic input distributions and varied ML model types.

title: VulWeaver: Weaving Broken Semantics for Grounded Vulnerability Detection
summary: Detecting vulnerabilities in source code remains critical yet challenging, as conventional static analysis tools construct inaccurate program representations, while existing LLM-based approaches often miss essential vulnerability context and lack grounded reasoning. To mitigate these challenges, we introduce VulWeaver, a novel LLM-based approach that weaves broken program semantics into accurate representations and extracts holistic vulnerability context for grounded vulnerability detection. Specifically, VulWeaver first constructs an enhanced unified dependency graph (UDG) by integrating deterministic rules with LLM-based semantic inference to address static analysis inaccuracies. It then extracts holistic vulnerability context by combining explicit contexts from program slicing with implicit contexts, including usage, definition, and declaration information. Finally, VulWeaver employs meta-prompting with vulnerability type specific expert guidelines to steer LLMs through systematic reasoning, aggregated via majority voting for robustness. Extensive experiments on PrimeVul4J dataset have demonstrated that VulWeaver achieves a F1-score of 0.75, outperforming state-of-the-art learning-based, LLM-based, and agent-based baselines by 23%, 15%, and 60% in F1-score, respectively. VulWeaver has also detected 26 true vulnerabilities across 9 realworld Java projects, with 15 confirmed by developers and 5 CVE identifiers assigned. In industrial deployment, VulWeaver identified 40 confirmed vulnerabilities in an internal repository.

AI.tldr: Detecting vulnerabilities in source code is challenging due to limitations in static analysis and LLM-based methods. VulWeaver introduces a new LLM-based system to improve vulnerability detection by enhancing program representations and applying systematic reasoning. It achieves a substantial F1-score improvement and real-world applicability.
AI.motivation: Current methods for detecting code vulnerabilities have limitations, such as inaccurate program representations and lack of context or reasoning. Enhancing effectiveness in this space is crucial for cybersecurity.
AI.method: VulWeaver creates an enhanced unified dependency graph (UDG) combining rules with LLM-based inference, extracts contextual vulnerability information using program slicing, and applies meta-prompting with type-specific guidelines. Aggregated results via majority voting ensure robustness.
AI.result: Experiments on PrimeVul4J dataset showed VulWeaver achieved an F1-score of 0.75, outperforming top baselines by 15%-60%. It also identified confirmed vulnerabilities in both real-world projects and industrial applications, with CVE identifiers assigned.
AI.conclusion: VulWeaver significantly advances LLM-based vulnerability detection with enhanced accuracy, robust reasoning, and proven real-world effectiveness, addressing critical challenges in existing approaches.

title: AffordGen: Generating Diverse Demonstrations for Generalizable Object Manipulation with Afford Correspondence
summary: Despite the recent success of modern imitation learning methods in robot manipulation, their performance is often constrained by geometric variations due to limited data diversity. Leveraging powerful 3D generative models and vision foundation models (VFMs), the proposed AffordGen framework overcomes this limitation by utilizing the semantic correspondence of meaningful keypoints across large-scale 3D meshes to generate new robot manipulation trajectories. This large-scale, affordance-aware dataset is then used to train a robust, closed-loop visuomotor policy, combining the semantic generalizability of affordances with the reactive robustness of end-to-end learning. Experiments in simulation and the real world show that policies trained with AffordGen achieve high success rates and enable zero-shot generalization to truly unseen objects, significantly improving data efficiency in robot learning.

AI.tldr: The AffordGen framework uses 3D generative models and vision foundation models to enhance robot manipulation by generating diverse trajectories, improving performance on unseen objects.
AI.motivation: To address performance constraints in robot manipulation caused by limited data diversity and geometric variations in conventional imitation learning methods.
AI.method: AffordGen creates a dataset by leveraging semantic keypoints on 3D meshes, enabling the generation of diverse manipulation trajectories. This dataset is used to train a robust visuomotor policy that combines affordance generality with end-to-end learning's robustness.
AI.result: Experiments show high success rates for the trained policies in simulations and real-world scenarios, showcasing zero-shot generalization to unseen objects and improved data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates significant advancements in robot manipulation, addressing diversity limitations and enabling more robust and generalized learning methods.

title: Training-Free Cross-Lingual Dysarthria Severity Assessment via Phonological Subspace Analysis in Self-Supervised Speech Representations
summary: Dysarthric speech severity assessment typically requires trained clinicians or supervised models built from labelled pathological speech, limiting scalability across languages and clinical settings. We present a training-free method that quantifies dysarthria severity by measuring degradation in phonological feature subspaces within frozen HuBERT representations. No supervised severity model is trained; feature directions are estimated from healthy control speech using a pretrained forced aligner. For each speaker, we extract phone-level embeddings via Montreal Forced Aligner, compute d-prime scores along phonological contrast directions (nasality, voicing, stridency, sonorance, manner, and four vowel features) derived exclusively from healthy controls, and construct a 12-dimensional phonological profile.Evaluating 890 speakers across 10 corpora, 5 languages (English, Spanish, Dutch, Mandarin, French), and 3 primary aetiologies (Parkinson's disease, cerebral palsy, ALS), we find that all five consonant d-prime features correlate significantly with clinical severity (random-effects meta-analysis rho = -0.50 to -0.56, p < 2e-4; pooled Spearman rho = -0.47 to -0.55 with bootstrap 95% CIs not crossing zero). The effect replicates within individual corpora, survives FDR correction, and remains robust to leave-one-corpus-out removal and alignment quality controls. Nasality d-prime decreases monotonically from control to severe in 6 of 7 severity-graded corpora. Mann-Whitney U tests confirm that all 12 features distinguish controls from severely dysarthric speakers (p < 0.001).The method requires no dysarthric training data and applies to any language with an existing MFA acoustic model (currently 29 languages). We release the full pipeline and phone feature configurations for six languages.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a training-free method for assessing dysarthric speech severity by analyzing phonological degradation in frozen HuBERT embeddings without requiring labeled pathological speech data.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to assess dysarthria severity rely on trained clinicians or supervised models tied to labeled pathological speech, limiting scalability across languages and clinical scenarios.
AI.method: The paper utilizes frozen HuBERT speech embeddings and phonological feature directions derived from healthy controls using a pretrained forced aligner to build a 12-dimensional phonological profile for evaluating dysarthria.
AI.result: The study evaluated 890 speakers across 10 datasets, spanning 5 languages and 3 disorders. The method achieved significant correlations with clinical severity and robustness across various corpora and conditions.
AI.conclusion: This training-free approach works across multiple languages and eliminates the need for dysarthric training data. The authors provide their pipeline and configuration for broader application.

title: Evaluating Reliability Gaps in Large Language Model Safety via Repeated Prompt Sampling
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures; accepted at the CCAI 2026 conference
summary: Traditional benchmarks for large language models (LLMs), such as HELM and AIR-BENCH, primarily assess safety risk through breadth-oriented evaluation across diverse tasks. However, real-world deployment often exposes a different class of risk: operational failures arising from repeated generations of the same prompt rather than broad task generalization. In high-stakes settings, response consistency and safety under repeated use are critical operational requirements. We introduce Accelerated Prompt Stress Testing (APST), a depth-oriented evaluation framework inspired by highly accelerated stress testing in reliability engineering. APST probes LLM behavior by repeatedly sampling identical prompts under controlled operational conditions, including temperature variation and prompt perturbation, to surface latent failure modes such as hallucinations, refusal inconsistency, and unsafe completions. Rather than treating failures as isolated events, APST characterizes them statistically as stochastic outcomes of repeated inference. We model observed safety failures using Bernoulli and binomial formulations to estimate per-inference failure probabilities, enabling quantitative comparison of operational risk across models and configurations. We apply APST to multiple instruction-tuned LLMs evaluated on AIR-BENCH 2024 derived safety and security prompts. While models exhibit similar performance under conventional single- or very-low-sample evaluation (N <= 3), repeated sampling reveals substantial variation in empirical failure probabilities across temperatures. These results demonstrate that shallow benchmark scores can obscure meaningful differences in reliability under sustained use.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Accelerated Prompt Stress Testing (APST), a depth-oriented evaluation framework for identifying latent failure modes in large language models through repeated sampling of the same prompt.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLM benchmarks focus on breadth-oriented evaluations but overlook operational risks stemming from repeated use of identical prompts in real-world applications. This paper aims to address that gap.
AI.method: APST probes LLM behavior by repeatedly sampling identical prompts under controlled conditions, using statistical models to quantify failure rates. It compares operational risks across models and configurations.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation using APST revealed substantial variations in failure probabilities across temperatures, even among models performing similarly under conventional evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Shallow benchmark evaluations can mask significant reliability issues in LLMs under sustained use. APST provides a more nuanced understanding of operational risks.

title: Orthogonal Quadratic Complements for Vision Transformer Feed-Forward Networks
summary:
Recent bilinear feed-forward replacements for vision transformers can substantially improve accuracy, but they often conflate two effects: stronger second-order interactions and increased redundancy relative to the main branch. We study a complementary design principle in which auxiliary quadratic features contribute only information not already captured by the dominant hidden representation. To this end, we propose Orthogonal Quadratic Complements (OQC), which construct a low-rank quadratic auxiliary branch and explicitly project it onto the orthogonal complement of the main branch before injection. We further study an efficient low-rank realization (OQC-LR) and gated extensions (OQC-static and OQC-dynamic).
  Under a parameter-matched Deep-ViT and CIFAR-100 protocol with a fixed penultimate residual readout, full OQC improves an AFBO baseline from 64.25 +/- 0.22 to 65.59 +/- 0.22, while OQC-LR reaches 65.52 +/- 0.25 with a substantially better speed-accuracy tradeoff. On TinyImageNet, the gated extension OQC-dynamic achieves 51.88 +/- 0.32, improving the baseline (50.45 +/- 0.21) by 1.43 points and outperforming all ungated variants. Mechanism analyses show near-zero post-projection auxiliary-main overlap together with improved representation geometry and class separation. The full family, including both ungated and gated variants, generalizes consistently across both datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Orthogonal Quadratic Complements (OQC), a novel method for enhancing vision transformer accuracy by injecting quadratic features orthogonal to main branch representations.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the conflation in recent bilinear feed-forward replacements for vision transformers that mix second-order interactions and redundancy, by introducing complementary quadratic features in a structured manner.
AI.method: OQC constructs a low-rank quadratic auxiliary branch, projecting it onto the orthogonal complement of the main branch before injection. Extensions include efficient low-rank realization (OQC-LR) and gated variants (static and dynamic).
AI.result: OQC achieves notable accuracy improvements over baselines on CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet datasets (e.g., from 64.25% to 65.59% on CIFAR-100 with full OQC). Gated extensions outperform ungated variants.
AI.conclusion: Orthogonal projection-based quadratic feature designs improve performance, representation geometry, and class separation, offering consistent generalization across datasets.

title: K-STEMIT: Knowledge-Informed Spatio-Temporal Efficient Multi-Branch Graph Neural Network for Subsurface Stratigraphy Thickness Estimation from Radar Data
summary: Subsurface stratigraphy contains important spatio-temporal information about accumulation, deformation, and layer formation in polar ice sheets. In particular, variations in internal ice layer thickness provide valuable constraints for snow mass balance estimation and projections of ice sheet change. Although radar sensors can capture these layered structures as depth-resolved radargrams, convolutional neural networks applied directly to radar images are often sensitive to speckle noise and acquisition artifacts. In addition, purely data-driven methods may underuse physical knowledge, leading to unrealistic thickness estimates under spatial or temporal extrapolation. To address these challenges, we develop K-STEMIT, a novel knowledge-informed, efficient, multi-branch spatio-temporal graph neural network that combines a geometric framework for spatial learning with temporal convolution to capture temporal dynamics, and incorporates physical data synchronized from the Model Atmospheric Regional physical weather model. An adaptive feature fusion strategy is employed to dynamically combine features learned from different branches. Extensive experiments have been conducted to compare K-STEMIT against current state-of-the-art methods in both knowledge-informed and non-knowledge-informed settings, as well as other existing methods. Results show that K-STEMIT consistently achieves the highest accuracy while maintaining near-optimal efficiency. Most notably, incorporating adaptive feature fusion and physical priors reduces the root mean-squared error by 21.01% with negligible additional cost compared to its conventional multi-branch variants. Additionally, our proposed K-STEMIT achieves consistently lower per-year relative MAE, enabling reliable, continuous spatiotemporal assessment of snow accumulation variability across large spatial regions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces K-STEMIT, a graph neural network for analyzing ice sheet layer thickness using radar data, achieving higher accuracy and efficiency by integrating physical priors and adaptive feature fusion.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing methods in analyzing subsurface stratigraphy, especially issues related to noise, artifacts, and insufficient utilization of physical knowledge.
AI.method: The authors propose K-STEMIT, a multi-branch spatio-temporal graph neural network that combines spatial learning, temporal convolution, and physical data from atmospheric models. An adaptive feature fusion strategy is also introduced.
AI.result: K-STEMIT outperforms state-of-the-art methods with the highest accuracy and reduces root mean-squared error by 21.01%, maintaining efficiency. It provides lower per-year relative MAE, enabling reliable snow accumulation assessment.
AI.conclusion: The K-STEMIT model proves effective for spatiotemporal analysis of ice layers, combining data-driven techniques with physical knowledge to ensure realistic and accurate results.

title: Beyond Fixed False Discovery Rates: Post-Hoc Conformal Selection with E-Variables
summary: Conformal selection (CS) uses calibration data to identify test inputs whose unobserved outcomes are likely to satisfy a pre-specified minimal quality requirement, while controlling the false discovery rate (FDR). Existing methods fix the target FDR level before observing data, which prevents the user from adapting the balance between number of selected test inputs and FDR to downstream needs and constraints based on the available data. For example, in genomics or neuroimaging, researchers often inspect the distribution of test statistics, and decide how aggressively to pursue candidates based on observed evidence strength and available follow-up resources. To address this limitation, we introduce {post-hoc CS} (PH-CS), which generates a path of candidate selection sets, each paired with a data-driven false discovery proportion (FDP) estimate. PH-CS lets the user select any operating point on this path by maximizing a user-specified utility, arbitrarily balancing selection size and FDR. Building on conformal e-variables and the e-Benjamini-Hochberg (e-BH) procedure, PH-CS is proved to provide a finite-sample post-hoc reliability guarantee whereby the ratio between estimated FDP level and true FDP is, on average, upper bounded by $1$, so that the average estimated FDP is, to first order, a valid upper bound on the true FDR. PH-CS is extended to control quality defined in terms of a general risk. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that, unlike CS, PH-CS can consistently satisfy user-imposed utility constraints while producing reliable FDP estimates and maintaining competitive FDR control.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces post-hoc conformal selection (PH-CS), a framework that adapts selection size and false discovery rate (FDR) to user-defined preferences while providing valid FDR guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing conformal selection methodologies fix target FDR levels prior to observing the data, limiting adaptability in domains such as genomics or neuroimaging, where the balance between selection size and FDR can depend on observed evidence or available resources.
AI.method: The authors propose PH-CS, which produces a sequence of candidate selection sets paired with data-driven false discovery proportion (FDP) estimates. It builds on conformal e-variables and the e-Benjamini-Hochberg procedure to provide flexibly adaptable selection processes with post-hoc reliability guarantees.
AI.result: PH-CS achieves user-specified trade-offs between selection size and FDR, provides valid FDP estimate guarantees, and demonstrates consistent performance in synthetic and real-world datasets while maintaining reliable FDR control.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PH-CS framework overcomes limitations of traditional conformal selection by enabling flexible, post-hoc adjustments to selection criteria and delivering competitive reliability and performance.

title: Enhancing Understandability and Transparency of Research Software: Tracing Research to Code
comment: Accepted at the 1st International Workshop on Software Engineering and Research Software (SERS 2026)
summary: Modern research heavily relies on software. A significant challenge researchers face is understanding the complex software used in specific research fields. We target two scenarios in this context, namely long onboarding times for newcomers and conference reviewers evaluating replication packages. We hypothesize that both scenarios can be significantly improved when there is a clear link between the paper's ideas and the code that implements them. As a time- and staff-saving approach, we propose an LLM-based automation tool that takes in a paper and the software implementing the paper, and generates a trace mapping between research ideas and their locations in code. Initial experiments have shown that the tool can generate quite useful mappings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an automation tool using large language models (LLMs) to link research ideas with their code implementation, addressing onboarding and code-review challenges.
AI.motivation: Researchers face difficulties understanding complex software in research, especially during onboarding and reviewing replication packages. The authors aim to simplify linking ideas to code.
AI.method: They propose an LLM-based tool that automatically maps research ideas from papers to their corresponding locations in the implementing software.
AI.result: Initial experiments indicated that the tool produces useful mappings between research concepts and code.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tool has the potential to improve accessibility and efficiency in research understanding and evaluation processes.

title: MonoEM-GS: Monocular Expectation-Maximization Gaussian Splatting SLAM
summary: Feed-forward geometric foundation models can infer dense point clouds and camera motion directly from RGB streams, providing priors for monocular SLAM. However, their predictions are often view-dependent and noisy: geometry can vary across viewpoints and under image transformations, and local metric properties may drift between frames. We present MonoEM-GS, a monocular mapping pipeline that integrates such geometric predictions into a global Gaussian Splatting representation while explicitly addressing these inconsistencies. MonoEM-GS couples Gaussian Splatting with an Expectation--Maximization formulation to stabilize geometry, and employs ICP-based alignment for monocular pose estimation. Beyond geometry, MonoEM-GS parameterizes Gaussians with multi-modal features, enabling in-place open-set segmentation and other downstream queries directly on the reconstructed map. We evaluate MonoEM-GS on 7-Scenes, TUM RGB-D and Replica, and compare against recent baselines.

AI.tldr: MonoEM-GS enhances monocular mapping by integrating geometric predictions into a global Gaussian Splatting representation and addresses inconsistencies in dense point clouds and camera motion.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to mitigate view-dependent noise and inconsistencies in geometry predictions from feed-forward geometric foundation models for robust monocular SLAM applications.
AI.method: The authors introduce MonoEM-GS, which uses Gaussian Splatting coupled with Expectation--Maximization to stabilize geometry, and ICP-based alignment for pose estimation, while supporting multi-modal feature parameterization for advanced queries.
AI.result: MonoEM-GS was evaluated on multiple datasets (7-Scenes, TUM RGB-D, Replica) and outperformed recent baselines in monocular SLAM tasks.
AI.conclusion: MonoEM-GS provides a robust way to use geometric predictions for creating consistent monocular maps, with added benefits of feature-rich downstream queries and improved pose estimation.

title: Think in Sentences: Explicit Sentence Boundaries Enhance Language Model's Capabilities
summary: Researchers have explored different ways to improve large language models (LLMs)' capabilities via dummy token insertion in contexts. However, existing works focus solely on the dummy tokens themselves, but fail to leverage the inherent sentence-level structure of natural language. This is a critical oversight, as LLMs acquire linguistic capabilities through exposure to human-generated texts, which are inherently structured at the sentence level. Motivated by this gap, we propose an approach that inserts delimiters at sentence boundaries in LLM inputs, which not only integrates dummy tokens into the context, but also facilitates LLMs with sentence-by-sentence processing behavior during reasoning. Two concrete methods: (1). In-context learning and (2). Supervised fine-tuning are experimented using 7B models to 600B Deepseek-V3. Our results demonstrate consistent improvements across various tasks, with notable gains of up to 7.7\% on GSM8k and 12.5\% on DROP. Furthermore, the fine-tuned LLMs can incorporate sentence awareness evidenced by their internal representations. Our work establishes a simple yet effective technique for enhancing LLM's capabilities, offering promising directions for cognitive-inspired LLM enhancement paradigm.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes inserting delimiters at sentence boundaries in LLM inputs to improve reasoning and performance across tasks. Experiments show significant gains on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches that use dummy tokens in LLMs neglect sentence-level natural language structures, which are key to LLMs' linguistic understanding.
AI.method: The authors suggest sentence delimiters for sentence-by-sentence reasoning via two approaches: in-context learning and supervised fine-tuning, experimented on models ranging from 7B to 600B parameters.
AI.result: Improvements up to 7.7% on GSM8k and 12.5% on DROP benchmarks, with sentence awareness evident in fine-tuned models' internal representations.
AI.conclusion: A simple yet impactful technique, sentence-boundary delimiters advance LLM cognitive performance, offering a novel enhancement paradigm.

title: Unifying Ontology Construction and Semantic Alignment for Deterministic Enterprise Reasoning at Scale
summary: While enterprises amass vast quantities of data, much of it remains chaotic and effectively dormant, preventing decision-making based on comprehensive information. Existing neuro-symbolic approaches rely on disjoint pipelines and struggle with error propagation. We introduce the large ontology model (LOM), a unified framework that seamlessly integrates ontology construction, semantic alignment, and logical reasoning into a single end-to-end architecture. LOM employs a construct-align-reason (CAR) pipeline, leveraging its unified architecture across all three stages: it first autonomously constructs a domain-specific ontological universe from raw data, then aligns neural generation with this structural reality using a graph-aware encoder and reinforcement learning, and finally executes deterministic reasoning over the constructed topology, node attributes and relation types. We evaluate LOM on a comprehensive benchmark constructed from diverse real-world enterprise datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that LOM-4B achieves 88.8% accuracy in ontology completion and 94% in complex graph reasoning tasks, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art LLMs. These findings validate that autonomous logical construction is essential for achieving deterministic, enterprise-grade intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a unified framework, LOM, which integrates ontology construction, semantic alignment, and logical reasoning for enterprise data, achieving superior results compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Enterprises struggle to utilize vast amounts of chaotic and dormant data effectively due to limitations in existing neuro-symbolic approaches, which have disjointed pipelines and error propagation.
AI.method: The paper presents a construct-align-reason (CAR) pipeline within the LOM framework, which autonomously builds domain-specific ontology from raw data, aligns neural generation to structural graphs using a graph-aware encoder and reinforcement learning, and executes deterministic reasoning.
AI.result: LOM-4B achieves 88.8% accuracy in ontology completion and 94% accuracy in complex graph reasoning tasks, surpassing current state-of-the-art large language models.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous logical construction within a unified framework, as seen in LOM, is critical for robust, deterministic intelligence in enterprise contexts.

title: Robust Fair Disease Diagnosis in CT Images
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at the 3rd Workshop on New Trends in AI-Generated Media and Security (AIMS) @ CVPR 2026
summary: Automated diagnosis from chest CT has improved considerably with deep learning, but models trained on skewed datasets tend to perform unevenly across patient demographics. However, the situation is worse than simple demographic bias. In clinical data, class imbalance and group underrepresentation often coincide, creating compound failure modes that neither standard rebalancing nor fairness corrections can fix alone. We introduce a two-level objective that targets both axes of this problem. Logit-adjusted cross-entropy loss operates at the sample level, shifting decision margins by class frequency with provable consistency guarantees. Conditional Value at Risk aggregation operates at the group level, directing optimization pressure toward whichever demographic group currently has the higher loss. We evaluate on the Fair Disease Diagnosis benchmark using a 3D ResNet-18 pretrained on Kinetics-400, classifying CT volumes into Adenocarcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, COVID-19, and Normal groups with patient sex annotations. The training set illustrates the compound problem concretely: squamous cell carcinoma has 84 samples total, 5 of them female. The combined loss reaches a gender-averaged macro F1 of 0.8403 with a fairness gap of 0.0239, a 13.3% improvement in score and 78% reduction in demographic disparity over the baseline. Ablations show that each component alone falls short. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/Purdue-M2/Fair-Disease-Diagnosis.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a two-level loss objective to address biases in automated chest CT diagnosis caused by class imbalance and demographic underrepresentation. It achieves notable improvements in fairness and performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation lies in addressing compounded biases where class imbalance and demographic underrepresentation coexist in medical datasets, causing failure modes that traditional balancing methods cannot resolve.
AI.method: The authors present a combined two-level objective: Logit-adjusted cross-entropy loss for class frequency rebalancing and Conditional Value at Risk aggregation for fairness across demographic groups. Evaluation on a 3D ResNet-18 setup assesses its impact.
AI.result: The method achieves a gender-averaged macro F1 score of 0.8403 with a fairness gap of 0.0239, showing a 13.3% improvement in score and a 78% reduction in demographic disparity compared to the baseline.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses both class imbalance and demographic disparity, offering significant advancements in fairness and diagnostic accuracy. Code availability ensures reproducibility of results.

title: A Hybrid Intelligent Framework for Uncertainty-Aware Condition Monitoring of Industrial Systems
summary: Hybrid approaches that combine data-driven learning with physics-based insight have shown promise for improving the reliability of industrial condition monitoring. This work develops a hybrid condition monitoring framework that integrates primary sensor measurements, lagged temporal features, and physics-informed residuals derived from nominal surrogate models. Two hybrid integration strategies are examined. The first is a feature-level fusion approach that augments the input space with residual and temporal information. The second is a model-level ensemble approach in which machine learning classifiers trained on different feature types are combined at the decision level. Both hybrid approaches of the condition monitoring framework are evaluated on a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) benchmark using several machine learning models and ensemble configurations. Both feature-level and model-level hybridization improve diagnostic accuracy relative to single-source baselines, with the best model-level ensemble achieving a 2.9\% improvement over the best baseline ensemble. To assess predictive reliability, conformal prediction is applied to quantify coverage, prediction-set size, and abstention behavior. The results show that hybrid integration enhances uncertainty management, producing smaller and well-calibrated prediction sets at matched coverage levels. These findings demonstrate that lightweight physics-informed residuals, temporal augmentation, and ensemble learning can be combined effectively to improve both accuracy and decision reliability in nonlinear industrial systems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a hybrid condition-monitoring framework that combines machine learning with physics insights to improve diagnostic accuracy and predictive reliability.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enhance the reliability and uncertainty management of industrial condition-monitoring systems by leveraging both data-driven learning and physics-based insights.
AI.method: The framework integrates primary sensor data, temporal features, and physics-informed residuals. Two approaches are analyzed: feature-level fusion and model-level ensemble. Conformal prediction is used for assessing uncertainty.
AI.result: Both hybrid approaches outperform single-source methods. The best model-level ensemble achieves a 2.9% improvement in diagnostic accuracy, with enhanced uncertainty management via prediction sets.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid integration effectively improves both accuracy and reliability in condition monitoring for nonlinear industrial systems, validating the synergy of physics-informed strategies with machine learning.

title: ConfigSpec: Profiling-Based Configuration Selection for Distributed Edge--Cloud Speculative LLM Serving
comment: 6 Pages, 6 figures, accepted by the 4th International Workshop on Testing Distributed Internet of Things Systems (TDIS 2026)
summary: Speculative decoding enables collaborative Large Language Model (LLM) inference across cloud and edge by separating lightweight token drafting from heavyweight verification. While prior systems show performance and cost benefits, practical deployment requires navigating a large configuration space spanning draft model variants, quantisation levels, speculative lengths, and heterogeneous edge devices. This paper presents ConfigSpec, a configurationselection framework for distributed speculative LLM serving. ConfigSpec profiles edge devices and draft-target alignment, and models drafting throughput, acceptance rate, and power to evaluate goodput, verification cost efficiency, and energy efficiency across the joint configuration space. Our analysis across three edge platforms and two LLM families reveals structurally conflicting optima. Firstly, goodput is maximised by the smallest, fastest draft model at device-dependent speculative lengths (K*=2-10). Secondly, both cost and energy efficiency converge to K=2 due to a dominant bonus-token effect-with cost favouring the largest drafter for its high acceptance rate and energy favouring the smallest for its low power draw. These conflicts confirm that no single fixed configuration can simultaneously optimise all objectives, underscoring the need for profiling-based configuration selection in disaggregated edge-cloud LLM inference.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ConfigSpec, a framework for optimizing speculative decoding in Large Language Model (LLM) deployment across cloud and edge devices, highlighting conflicting configuration optima for goodput, cost, and energy efficiency.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding for LLMs offers performance and cost advantages by splitting token generation into lightweight drafting and heavy verification phases. However, deploying such systems practically is complex due to the vast configuration space involving device capabilities, model variants, and operational factors.
AI.method: ConfigSpec profiles edge devices and model alignments to analyze drafting throughput, acceptance rate, and power consumption. It evaluates goodput, verification cost efficiency, and energy efficiency across configurations to identify optimal settings for distributed LLM systems.
AI.result: The study finds that optimal configurations for goodput, cost efficiency, and energy efficiency conflict structurally. Goodput is highest with small, fast drafting models, while cost and energy efficiency benefit from different draft model sizes and speculative lengths. These conflicts highlight the necessity of tailored configuration selections.
AI.conclusion: No universal configuration can simultaneously maximize goodput, cost, and energy efficiency. Profiling and adaptive configuration selection are essential for practical and efficient LLM inference in edge-cloud setups.

title: Learning Discrete Diffusion of Graphs via Free-Energy Gradient Flows
summary: Diffusion-based models on continuous spaces have seen substantial recent progress through the mathematical framework of gradient flows, leveraging the Wasserstein-2 (${W}_2$) metric via the Jordan-Kinderlehrer-Otto (JKO) scheme. Despite the increasing popularity of diffusion models on discrete spaces using continuous-time Markov chains, a parallel theoretical framework based on gradient flows has remained elusive due to intrinsic challenges in translating the ${W}_2$ distance directly into these settings. In this work, we propose the first computational approach addressing these challenges, leveraging an appropriate metric $W_K$ on the simplex of probability distributions, which enables us to interpret widely used discrete diffusion paths, such as the discrete heat equation, as gradient flows of specific free-energy functionals. Through this theoretical insight, we introduce a novel methodology for learning diffusion dynamics over discrete spaces, which recovers the underlying functional directly by leveraging first-order optimality conditions for the JKO scheme. The resulting method optimizes a simple quadratic loss, trains extremely fast, does not require individual sample trajectories, and only needs a numerical preprocessing computing $W_K$-geodesics. We validate our method through extensive numerical experiments on synthetic data, showing that we can recover the underlying functional for a variety of graph classes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel theoretical and computational framework for diffusion-based models in discrete spaces using the Wasserstein-K metric to define gradient flows, enabling efficient recovery of diffusion dynamics via a faster training method.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion models in discrete spaces lack a theoretical framework based on gradient flows, largely due to the difficulty of applying the Wasserstein-2 distance in these contexts.
AI.method: The authors define a new metric $W_K$ on the simplex of probability distributions, enabling the interpretation of diffusion paths as gradient flows. They use the JKO scheme's first-order optimality conditions to recover diffusion dynamics, optimizing a quadratic loss in the process.
AI.result: The proposed method accurately recovers the underlying functional of diffusion dynamics on various graph classes, performs numerical preprocessing of $W_K$-geodesics, and achieves efficient training without requiring individual sample trajectories.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a pioneering computational framework for gradient flows in discrete spaces, showcasing theoretical insights and practical results that generalize and enhance diffusion modeling approaches.

title: AWARE: Adaptive Whole-body Active Rotating Control for Enhanced LiDAR-Inertial Odometry under Human-in-the-Loop Interaction
summary: Human-in-the-loop (HITL) UAV operation is essential in complex and safety-critical aerial surveying environments, where human operators provide navigation intent while onboard autonomy must maintain accurate and robust state estimation. A key challenge in this setting is that resource-constrained UAV platforms are often limited to narrow-field-of-view LiDAR sensors. In geometrically degenerate or feature-sparse scenes, limited sensing coverage often weakens LiDAR Inertial Odometry (LIO)'s observability, causing drift accumulation, degraded geometric accuracy, and unstable state estimation, which directly compromise safe and effective HITL operation and the reliability of downstream surveying products. To overcome this limitation, we present AWARE, a bio-inspired whole-body active yawing framework that exploits the UAV's own rotational agility to extend the effective sensor horizon and improve LIO's observability without additional mechanical actuation. The core of AWARE is a differentiable Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework embedded in a Reinforcement Learning (RL) loop. It first identifies the viewing direction that maximizes information gain across the full yaw space, and a lightweight RL agent then adjusts the MPC cost weights online according to the current environmental context, enabling an adaptive balance between estimation accuracy and flight stability. A Safe Flight Corridor mechanism further ensures operational safety within this HITL paradigm by decoupling the operator's navigational intent from autonomous yaw optimization to enable safe and efficient cooperative control. We validate AWARE through extensive experiments in diverse simulated and real-world environments.

AI.tldr: The paper presents AWARE, a bio-inspired active yawing framework for UAVs to enhance LiDAR Inertial Odometry (LIO) accuracy in resource-constrained sensing environments.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in UAV operations in feature-sparse scenes where limited LiDAR coverage leads to degraded state estimation and reduced safety and accuracy in human-in-the-loop UAV operations.
AI.method: AWARE uses a combination of Model Predictive Control (MPC) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) to maximize information gain via active yawing, ensuring adaptability and safety, while decoupling navigational intent from yaw optimization.
AI.result: AWARE was validated with extensive experiments in both simulated and real environments, showing improved state estimation accuracy and flight stability.
AI.conclusion: AWARE improves UAV state estimation and operational safety in complex environments, enabling better HITL performance without additional mechanical hardware.

title: Nationality encoding in language model hidden states: Probing culturally differentiated representations in persona-conditioned academic text
summary: Large language models are increasingly used as writing tools and pedagogical resources in English for Academic Purposes, but it remains unclear whether they encode culturally differentiated representations when generating academic text. This study tests whether Gemma-3-4b-it encodes nationality-discriminative information in hidden states when generating research article introductions conditioned by British and Chinese academic personas. A corpus of 270 texts was generated from 45 prompt templates crossed with six persona conditions in a 2 x 3 design. Logistic regression probes were trained on hidden-state activations across all 35 layers, with shuffled-label baselines, a surface-text skyline classifier, cross-family tests, and sentence-level baselines used as controls. Probe-selected token positions were annotated for structural, lexical, and stance features using the Stanza NLP pipeline. The nationality probe reached 0.968 cross-validated accuracy at Layer 18, with perfect held-out classification. Nationality encoding followed a non-monotonic trajectory across layers, with structural effects strongest in the middle to upper network and lexical-domain effects peaking earlier. At high-signal token positions, British-associated patterns showed more postmodification, hedging, boosting, passive voice, and evaluative or process-oriented vocabulary, while Chinese-associated patterns showed more premodification, nominal predicates, and sociocultural or internationalisation vocabulary. However, sentence-level analysis found no significant nationality differences in the full generated surface text. The findings extend probing methodology to a sociolinguistic attribute and have practical implications for EAP and language pedagogy.

AI.tldr: This study finds that a large language model (Gemma-3-4b-it) encodes nationality-related information when writing academic text, even though such differences don't significantly appear in the final output.
AI.motivation: To test whether nationality-differentiated academic writing styles can be encoded by large language models, particularly when used for English for Academic Purposes (EAP).
AI.method: Generated 270 texts using prompts styled as British and Chinese academic personas, applied logistic regression probes to the model's hidden states, and analyzed structural and lexical features with controls and NLP-based annotations.
AI.result: The model encoded nationality information in hidden states, achieving 96.8% accuracy at classification, with structural distinctions in British and Chinese styles, though surface-level generated text differences were nonsignificant.
AI.conclusion: Nationality-focused stylistic variations exist in the latent stages of the language model, which could inform future use in academic and pedagogical contexts.

title: General-purpose LLMs as Models of Human Driver Behavior: The Case of Simplified Merging
summary: Human behavior models are essential as behavior references and for simulating human agents in virtual safety assessment of automated vehicles (AVs), yet current models face a trade-off between interpretability and flexibility. General-purpose large language models (LLMs) offer a promising alternative: a single model potentially deployable without parameter fitting across diverse scenarios. However, what LLMs can and cannot capture about human driving behavior remains poorly understood. We address this gap by embedding two general-purpose LLMs (OpenAI o3 and Google Gemini 2.5 Pro) as standalone, closed-loop driver agents in a simplified one-dimensional merging scenario and comparing their behavior against human data using quantitative and qualitative analyses. Both models reproduce human-like intermittent operational control and tactical dependencies on spatial cues. However, neither consistently captures the human response to dynamic velocity cues, and safety performance diverges sharply between models. A systematic prompt ablation study reveals that prompt components act as model-specific inductive biases that do not transfer across LLMs. These findings suggest that general-purpose LLMs could potentially serve as standalone, ready-to-use human behavior models in AV evaluation pipelines, but future research is needed to better understand their failure modes and ensure their validity as models of human driving behavior.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how general-purpose large language models (LLMs) can serve as human driver behavior models in AV safety assessments, focusing on their capabilities and limitations in a merging scenario.
AI.motivation: The motivation of this study is to address the current trade-off between interpretability and flexibility in human behavior models used for AV evaluation. The authors explore whether LLMs can serve as scalable, ready-to-use models in diverse scenarios without parameter fitting.
AI.method: The researchers embedded two general-purpose LLMs (OpenAI o3 and Google Gemini 2.5 Pro) as standalone driver agents in a one-dimensional merging scenario. They performed both quantitative and qualitative comparisons between LLM behaviors and human driving behaviors, including a systematic prompt ablation study to analyze inductive biases.
AI.result: The study found that both LLMs replicated certain human-like behaviors, such as intermittent operational control and spatial dependencies. However, they failed to consistently respond to dynamic velocity cues, exhibited sharp divergences in safety performance, and displayed non-transferable inductive biases across different LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The research concludes that general-purpose LLMs hold potential as standalone human behavior models for AV evaluation. However, further research is required to understand their limitations, particularly their failure modes and inconsistencies, to validate them as reliable and accurate models of human driving behavior.

title: Head-wise Modality Specialization within MLLMs for Robust Fake News Detection under Missing Modality
summary: Multimodal fake news detection (MFND) aims to verify news credibility by jointly exploiting textual and visual evidence. However, real-world news dissemination frequently suffers from missing modality due to deleted images, corrupted screenshots, and similar issues. Thus, robust detection in this scenario requires preserving strong verification ability for each modality, which is challenging in MFND due to insufficient learning of the low-contribution modality and scarce unimodal annotations. To address this issue, we propose Head-wise Modality Specialization within Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for robust MFND under missing modality. Specifically, we first systematically study attention heads in MLLMs and their relationship with performance under missing modality, showing that modality-critical heads serve as key carriers of unimodal verification ability through their modality specialization. Based on this observation, to better preserve verification ability for the low-contribution modality, we introduce a head-wise specialization mechanism that explicitly allocates these heads to different modalities and preserves their specialization through lower-bound attention constraints. Furthermore, to better exploit scarce unimodal annotations, we propose a Unimodal Knowledge Retention strategy that prevents these heads from drifting away from the unimodal knowledge learned from limited supervision. Experiments show that our method improves robustness under missing modality while preserving performance with full multimodal input.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of detecting fake news when multimodal data (text and visuals) is incomplete by improving robust performance under missing modalities.
AI.motivation: To enhance the robustness of multimodal fake news detection (MFND) in real-world scenarios where one modality (text or visuals) may be missing due to issues like corrupted or deleted files.
AI.method: Developed a head-wise modality specialization mechanism in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to allocate attention heads to specific modalities and preserve their modality-specific knowledge using lower-bound attention constraints.
AI.result: The proposed approach improves the robustness of fake news detection under conditions of missing modality, while maintaining strong performance with full multimodal input.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods successfully address missing modality issues in MFND by leveraging attention head specialization and unimodal knowledge retention strategies.

title: Vestibular reservoir computing
summary: Reservoir computing (RC) is a computational framework known for its training efficiency, making it ideal for physical hardware implementations. However, realizing the complex interconnectivity of traditional reservoirs in physical systems remains a significant challenge. This paper proposes a physical RC scheme inspired by the biological vestibular system. To overcome hardware complexity, we introduce a designed uncoupled topology and demonstrate that it achieves performance comparable to fully coupled networks. We theoretically analyze the difference between these topologies by deriving a memory capacity formula for linear reservoirs, identifying specific conditions where both configurations yield equivalent memory. These analytical results are demonstrated to approximately hold for nonlinear reservoir systems. Furthermore, we systematically examine the impact of reservoir size on predictive statistics and memory capacity. Our findings suggest that uncoupled reservoir architectures offer a mathematically sound and practically feasible pathway for efficient physical reservoir computing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an uncoupled reservoir computing topology inspired by the biological vestibular system, achieving comparable performance to complex coupled networks.
AI.motivation: Reservoir computing is efficient for training and suitable for hardware, but its complex traditional connectivity poses implementation challenges.
AI.method: The paper designs an uncoupled reservoir topology, analyzes its memory capacity theoretically, and tests its feasibility in both linear and nonlinear cases.
AI.result: The uncoupled architecture performs similarly to coupled networks in predictive tasks, with equivalent memory under specific conditions.
AI.conclusion: Uncoupled reservoir architectures provide a simpler and effective alternative for physical reservoir computing frameworks.

title: SMART: When is it Actually Worth Expanding a Speculative Tree?
summary: Tree-based speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive generation by verifying a branching tree of draft tokens in a single target-model forward pass. However, existing methods prioritize maximizing token-level likelihood or the number of accepted tokens while ignoring a critical ``efficiency paradox'': the computational overhead of drafting and verifying big trees can grow super-linearly, particularly at scale. This often leads to negative wall-clock speedup when batch sizes increase or hardware saturation limits are reached. To address this, we propose SMART, a system-aware marginal analysis framework for runtime tree construction. SMART reformulates tree expansion as a hardware-aware optimization problem that directly maximizes end-to-end speedup. By applying a principled marginal benefit--cost rule at inference time, SMART expands a node only when its marginal benefit--cost ratio exceeds the tree-level speedup. SMART is training-free and serves as a plug-and-play controller for existing frameworks like MSD and EAGLE. Extensive evaluations across three MLLMs (e.g., LLaVA, Qwen2-VL) and four LLMs (e.g., Llama-3.1, DeepSeek-R1) demonstrate that SMART consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. It delivers an average additional speedup of 20.0\% for MLLMs and 15.4\% for LLMs across compute-bound batching regimes and diverse GPU architectures without performance loss.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SMART, an optimization framework that enhances tree-based speculative decoding for autoregressive models by addressing inefficiencies in runtime tree construction, significantly improving speedup across diverse architectures and models without performance degradation.
AI.motivation: Existing tree-based speculative decoding methods face efficiency issues due to the computational overhead of large trees, especially at high batch sizes or hardware limits, which reduces wall-clock speedup at scale.
AI.method: SMART applies a marginal benefit-cost rule at inference time to optimize runtime tree expansion. It maximizes end-to-end speedup by incorporating hardware-aware considerations. SMART is training-free and integrates seamlessly with current frameworks.
AI.result: Evaluations show SMART delivers an average additional speedup of 20.0% for multimodal language models (MLLMs) and 15.4% for large language models (LLMs), outperforming state-of-the-art approaches across GPUs and batching scenarios without losing performance.
AI.conclusion: SMART provides a hardware-aware, plug-and-play solution for optimizing tree-based speculative decoding, ensuring substantial speedup improvements while maintaining performance and scalability.

title: Deep Learning for Sequential Decision Making under Uncertainty: Foundations, Frameworks, and Frontiers
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is moving increasingly beyond prediction to support decisions in complex, uncertain, and dynamic environments. This shift creates a natural intersection with operations research and management sciences (OR/MS), which have long offered conceptual and methodological foundations for sequential decision-making under uncertainty. At the same time, recent advances in deep learning, including feedforward neural networks, LSTMs, transformers, and deep reinforcement learning, have expanded the scope of data-driven modeling and opened new possibilities for large-scale decision systems. This tutorial presents an OR/MS-centered perspective on deep learning for sequential decision-making under uncertainty. Its central premise is that deep learning is valuable not as a replacement for optimization, but as a complement to it. Deep learning brings adaptability and scalable approximation, whereas OR/MS provides the structural rigor needed to represent constraints, recourse, and uncertainty. The tutorial reviews key decision-making foundations, connects them to the major neural architectures in modern AI, and discusses leading approaches to integrating learning and optimization. It also highlights emerging impact in domains such as supply chains, healthcare and epidemic response, agriculture, energy, and autonomous operations. More broadly, it frames these developments as part of a wider transition from predictive AI toward decision-capable AI and highlights the role of OR/MS in shaping the next generation of integrated learning--optimization systems.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how artificial intelligence, particularly deep learning, intersects with operations research/management sciences (OR/MS) for sequential decision-making under uncertainty. It emphasizes AI as a complement to optimization, bridging adaptability with structural rigor.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in decision-capable AI by integrating deep learning methodologies with the proven frameworks of operations research and management sciences.
AI.method: The paper reviews deep learning architectures—like neural networks, transformers, and reinforcement learning—and links them with OR/MS strategies to create integrated systems for decision-making under uncertainty.
AI.result: Key insights into combining AI's flexibility with OR/MS's structured approaches are highlighted. Emerging applications in areas such as healthcare, supply chains, energy, and autonomous operations demonstrate tangible benefits.
AI.conclusion: This research establishes the foundation for a transition from predictive AI to decision-capable AI, underpinned by the integration of learning and optimization paradigms in OR/MS frameworks.

title: LLMs for Qualitative Data Analysis Fail on Security-specificComments in Human Experiments
summary: [Background:] Thematic analysis of free-text justifications in human experiments provides significant qualitative insights. Yet, it is costly because reliable annotations require multiple domain experts. Large language models (LLMs) seem ideal candidates to replace human annotators. [Problem:] Coding security-specific aspects (code identifiers mentioned, lines-of-code mentioned, security keywords mentioned) may require deeper contextual understanding than sentiment classification. [Objective:] Explore whether LLMs can act as automated annotators for technical security comments by human subjects. [Method:] We prompt four top-performing LLMs on LiveBench to detect nine security-relevant codes in free-text comments by human subjects analyzing vulnerable code snippets. Outputs are compared to human annotators using Cohen's Kappa (chance-corrected accuracy). We test different prompts mimicking annotation best practices, including emerging codes, detailed codebooks with examples, and conflicting examples. [Negative Results:] We observed marked improvements only when using detailed code descriptions; however, these improvements are not uniform across codes and are insufficient to reliably replace a human annotator. [Limitations:] Additional studies with more LLMs and annotation tasks are needed.

AI.tldr: LLMs were tested for coding security-specific aspects in technical comments, showing limited reliability compared to human annotators despite improving with detailed codes.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address challenges in qualitative thematic analysis of security-specific comments, where using multiple domain expert annotations is costly and difficult.
AI.method: Prompted four top-performing LLMs on LiveBench to identify nine security-relevant codes in comments, and evaluated performance using Cohen's Kappa, with tests on different annotation strategies.
AI.result: Detailed code descriptions improved LLM performance but results varied across codes and remained insufficient for reliably replacing human annotators.
AI.conclusion: LLMs have potential for assisting in thematic analysis but currently cannot fully substitute human expertise for nuanced security comment annotation tasks.

title: OmniUMI: Towards Physically Grounded Robot Learning via Human-Aligned Multimodal Interaction
summary: UMI-style interfaces enable scalable robot learning, but existing systems remain largely visuomotor, relying primarily on RGB observations and trajectory while providing only limited access to physical interaction signals. This becomes a fundamental limitation in contact-rich manipulation, where success depends on contact dynamics such as tactile interaction, internal grasping force, and external interaction wrench that are difficult to infer from vision alone. We present OmniUMI, a unified framework for physically grounded robot learning via human-aligned multimodal interaction. OmniUMI synchronously captures RGB, depth, trajectory, tactile sensing, internal grasping force, and external interaction wrench within a compact handheld system, while maintaining collection--deployment consistency through a shared embodiment design. To support human-aligned demonstration, OmniUMI provides dual-force feedback through bilateral gripper feedback and natural perception of external interaction wrench in the handheld embodiment. Built on this interface, we extend diffusion policy with visual, tactile, and force-related observations, and deploy the learned policy through impedance-based execution for unified regulation of motion and contact behavior. Experiments demonstrate reliable sensing and strong downstream performance on force-sensitive pick-and-place, interactive surface erasing, and tactile-informed selective release. Overall, OmniUMI combines physically grounded multimodal data acquisition with human-aligned interaction, providing a scalable foundation for learning contact-rich manipulation.

AI.tldr: OmniUMI is introduced as a robot learning framework leveraging multimodal data, including vision, tactile sensing, and force feedback, for more effective contact-rich manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing robot learning systems primarily use visual data and trajectories, lacking critical physical interaction signals like tactile and force dynamics crucial for contact-rich manipulation.
AI.method: The OmniUMI framework synchronously integrates multimodal data (RGB, depth, tactile, and force-related sensing) through a compact handheld system. It includes human-aligned features like dual-force feedback and impedance-based policy execution for contact behavior regulation.
AI.result: OmniUMI demonstrates strong downstream performance in tasks requiring nuanced physical interaction, such as force-sensitive pick-and-place and tactile-informed operations.
AI.conclusion: OmniUMI effectively integrates multimodal data with human-aligned interaction for scalable, versatile robot learning in manipulation tasks.

title: ODUTQA-MDC: A Task for Open-Domain Underspecified Tabular QA with Multi-turn Dialogue-based Clarification
comment: This paper has been accepted to the main conference of ACL 2026
summary: The advancement of large language models (LLMs) has enhanced tabular question answering (Tabular QA), yet they struggle with open-domain queries exhibiting underspecified or uncertain expressions. To address this, we introduce the ODUTQA-MDC task and the first comprehensive benchmark to tackle it. This benchmark includes: (1) a large-scale ODUTQA dataset with 209 tables and 25,105 QA pairs; (2) a fine-grained labeling scheme for detailed evaluation; and (3) a dynamic clarification interface that simulates user feedback for interactive assessment. We also propose MAIC-TQA, a multi-agent framework that excels at detecting ambiguities, clarifying them through dialogue, and refining answers. Experiments validate our benchmark and framework, establishing them as a key resource for advancing conversational, underspecification-aware Tabular QA research.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses challenges in open-domain tabular question answering (ODUTQA) and introduces a new benchmark, dataset, labeling scheme, and a multi-agent framework to improve the field.
AI.motivation: Large language models struggle with underspecified or uncertain tabular queries, highlighting the need for tools to address these limitations in ODUTQA.
