In this paper, we revisit both aspects. For the loss side, we propose a \emph{diff-based scale loss} that weights predictions according to the signed area difference between the predicted mask and the ground truth, yielding strictly monotonic gradients and stable convergence. We further analyze a family of four scale loss variants to understand how their geometric properties affect detection behavior. For the spatial side, we introduce \emph{Gaussian-shaped convolution} with a learnable scale parameter to match the center-concentrated intensity profile of infrared small targets, and augment it with a \emph{rotated pinwheel mask} that adaptively aligns the kernel with target orientation via a straight-through estimator. Extensive experiments on IRSTD-1k, NUDT-SIRST, and SIRST-UAVB demonstrate consistent improvements in $mIoU$, $P_d$, and $F_a$ over state-of-the-art methods. We release our anonymous code and pretrained models.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses challenges in infrared small target detection by introducing a diff-based scale loss for stable training and Gaussian-shaped convolution for improved spatial attention. Extensive experiments validate its effectiveness.
AI.motivation: The study aims to tackle training instability and inadequate spatial attention in infrared small target detection.
AI.method: The authors propose a diff-based scale loss for monotonic gradients and stable convergence, alongside Gaussian-shaped convolution with learnable parameters to improve spatial attention.
AI.result: Using datasets like IRSTD-1k and NUDT-SIRST, the proposed methods show consistent improvements in metric scores ($mIoU$, $P_d$, $F_a$) compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The methods improve detection performance, and the authors provide their code and pretrained models to encourage further research.

title: PepBenchmark: A Standardized Benchmark for Peptide Machine Learning
summary: Peptide therapeutics are widely regarded as the "third generation" of drugs, yet progress in peptide Machine Learning (ML) are hindered by the absence of standardized benchmarks. Here we present PepBenchmark, which unifies datasets, preprocessing, and evaluation protocols for peptide drug discovery. PepBenchmark comprises three components: (1) PepBenchData, a well-curated collection comprising 29 canonical-peptide and 6 non-canonical-peptide datasets across 7 groups, systematically covering key aspects of peptide drug development, representing, to the best of our knowledge, the most comprehensive AI-ready dataset resource to date; (2) PepBenchPipeline, a standardized preprocessing pipeline that ensures consistent dataset cleaning, construction, splitting, and feature transformation, mitigating quality issues common in ad hoc pipelines; and (3) PepBenchLeaderboard, a unified evaluation protocol and leaderboard with strong baselines across 4 major methodological families: Fingerprint-based, GNN-based, PLM-based, and SMILES-based models. Together, PepBenchmark provides the first standardized and comparable foundation for peptide drug discovery, facilitating methodological advances and translation into real-world applications. The data and code are publicly available at https://github.com/ZGCI-AI4S-Pep/PepBenchmark/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PepBenchmark, a standardized resource for peptide drug discovery that includes datasets, preprocessing pipelines, and evaluation protocols to overcome challenges in peptide machine learning.
AI.motivation: The absence of standardized benchmarks in peptide machine learning is a bottleneck hindering progress in peptide therapeutics, necessitating an integrated resource to support research and applications.
AI.method: The authors developed PepBenchmark, encompassing three main components: PepBenchData (a comprehensive dataset collection), PepBenchPipeline (standardized preprocessing protocols), and PepBenchLeaderboard (evaluation framework with baselines across model families).
AI.result: PepBenchmark offers the most comprehensive dataset for peptide drug discovery, addresses preprocessing inconsistencies, and provides a unified evaluation system to benchmark methodologies.
AI.conclusion: The establishment of PepBenchmark lays a standardized and robust foundation for advancing peptide machine learning research and translational applications, with all resources made publicly accessible.

title: Context Kubernetes: Declarative Orchestration of Enterprise Knowledge for Agentic AI Systems
comment: 24 pages, 8 tables, 1 figure, 8 experiments (5 correctness + 3 value). Open-source prototype: https://github.com/Cohorte-ai/context-kubernetes
summary: We introduce Context Kubernetes, an architecture for orchestrating enterprise knowledge in agentic AI systems, with a prototype implementation and eight experiments. The core observation is that delivering the right knowledge, to the right agent, with the right permissions, at the right freshness -- across an entire organization -- is structurally analogous to the container orchestration problem Kubernetes solved a decade ago. We formalize six core abstractions, a YAML-based declarative manifest for knowledge-architecture-as-code, a reconciliation loop, and a three-tier agent permission model where agent authority is always a strict subset of human authority. Three value experiments show: (1) without governance, agents serve phantom content from deleted sources and leak cross-domain data in 26.5% of queries; (2) without freshness monitoring, stale content is served silently -- with reconciliation, staleness is detected in under 1ms; (3) in five attack scenarios, flat permissions block 0/5 attacks, basic RBAC blocks 4/5, and the three-tier model blocks 5/5. Five correctness experiments confirm zero unauthorized deliveries, zero invariant violations, and architectural enforcement of out-of-band approval isolation that no surveyed enterprise platform provides. A survey of four major platforms (Microsoft, Salesforce, AWS, Google) documents that none architecturally isolates agent approval channels. We identify four properties that make context orchestration harder than container orchestration, and argue that these make the solution more valuable.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Context Kubernetes, an architecture for orchestrating enterprise knowledge in AI systems, comparing its role to Kubernetes for container orchestration, and tests its utility in various experiments.
AI.motivation: To better address the problem of delivering accurate and properly governed enterprise knowledge to AI agents, analogous to Kubernetes solving container orchestration challenges.
AI.method: Formalizing six core abstractions including YAML-manifest-based knowledge coding, creating a reconciliation loop, and implementing a three-tiered agent permission model while running eight experiments to test its governance and correctness.
AI.result: The architecture prevented unauthorized deliveries and governance issues, ensuring freshness and blocking all tested attacks with the three-tiered model. It highlighted four major properties complicating knowledge orchestration over container orchestration.
AI.conclusion: By designing an architecture with strict knowledge delivery rules and improved governance mechanisms, the approach surpasses existing platforms like Microsoft, AWS, or Salesforce in ensuring secure and effective AI knowledge orchestration.

title: ACT: Automated CPS Testing for Open-Source Robotic Platforms
summary: Open-source software for cyber-physical systems (CPS) often lacks robust testing involving robotic platforms, resulting in critical errors that remain undetected. This is especially challenging when multiple modules of CPS software are developed by various open-source contributors. To address this gap, we propose Automated CPS Testing (ACT) that performs automated, continuous testing of open-source software with its robotic platforms, integrated with the open-source infrastructure such as GitHub. We implement an ACT prototype and conduct a case study on an open-source CPS with an educational robotic platform to demonstrate its capabilities.

AI.tldr: Open-source CPS software often lacks robust testing with robotic platforms, leading to undetected critical errors. Automated CPS Testing (ACT) addresses this gap for better reliability.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to overcome the challenges of undetected errors in CPS software due to insufficient testing of robotic platforms, especially problematic in open-source systems developed by varied contributors.
AI.method: The authors introduce the Automated CPS Testing (ACT) approach, which integrates automated, continuous software testing with robotic platforms into open-source infrastructures, such as GitHub.
AI.result: An ACT prototype was developed and applied in a case study featuring an educational robotic platform, showcasing the system's operational capabilities.
AI.conclusion: ACT enhances error detection and reliability in open-source CPS software, validating the feasibility of automated and continuous testing with robotic platforms.

title: RCBSF: A Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Contract Revision via Stackelberg Game
summary: Despite the widespread adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) in Legal AI, their utility for automated contract revision remains impeded by hallucinated safety and a lack of rigorous behavioral constraints. To address these limitations, we propose the Risk-Constrained Bilevel Stackelberg Framework (RCBSF), which formulates revision as a non-cooperative Stackelberg game. RCBSF establishes a hierarchical Leader Follower structure where a Global Prescriptive Agent (GPA) imposes risk budgets upon a follower system constituted by a Constrained Revision Agent (CRA) and a Local Verification Agent (LVA) to iteratively optimize output. We provide theoretical guarantees that this bilevel formulation converges to an equilibrium yielding strictly superior utility over unguided configurations. Empirical validation on a unified benchmark demonstrates that RCBSF achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing iterative baselines with an average Risk Resolution Rate (RRR) of 84.21\% while enhancing token efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/xjiacs/RCBSF .

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a structured framework to improve automated contract revision using game theory, overcoming hallucination and behavioral constraint issues in LLMs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the problems of hallucinated safety and lack of strict constraints in automated contract revision via Legal AI.
AI.method: The method involves a Risk-Constrained Bilevel Stackelberg Framework (RCBSF) featuring a leader-follower game setup, composed of a Global Prescriptive Agent (GPA), Constrained Revision Agent (CRA), and Local Verification Agent (LVA).
AI.result: RCBSF achieves a Risk Resolution Rate (RRR) of 84.21%, surpassing iterative baselines and improving token efficiency, validated empirically on a benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively resolves key issues in contract revision, providing superior and efficient outcomes compared to unguided or baseline approaches.

title: A Dual-Positive Monotone Parameterization for Multi-Segment Bids and a Validity Assessment Framework for Reinforcement Learning Agent-based Simulation of Electricity Markets
summary: Reinforcement learning agent-based simulation (RL-ABS) has become an important tool for electricity market mechanism analysis and evaluation. In the modeling of monotone, bounded, multi-segment stepwise bids, existing methods typically let the policy network first output an unconstrained action and then convert it into a feasible bid curve satisfying monotonicity and boundedness through post-processing mappings such as sorting, clipping, or projection. However, such post-processing mappings often fail to satisfy continuous differentiability, injectivity, and invertibility at boundaries or kinks, thereby causing gradient distortion and leading to spurious convergence in simulation results. Meanwhile, most existing studies conduct mechanism analysis and evaluation mainly on the basis of training-curve convergence, without rigorously assessing the distance between the simulation outcomes and Nash equilibrium, which severely undermines the credibility of the results. To address these issues, this paper proposes...

AI.tldr: This paper identifies issues with reinforcement learning-based electricity market simulation methods, specifically in bid curve generation and outcome analysis, and proposes a new solution to address these shortcomings.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based approaches for electricity markets face challenges in generating feasible bid curves due to issues with traditional post-processing mappings, as well as lack of rigorous distance evaluation from Nash equilibrium in mechanism assessment.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the flaws in current methods, particularly around gradient distortion caused by post-processing mappings, and proposes an alternative approach to resolve these issues.
AI.result: The proposed solution improves both the generation of bid curves and the credibility of simulation outcomes by addressing the problems of non-differentiability and inadequate Nash equilibrium evaluation.
AI.conclusion: A novel methodology is introduced that significantly enhances the reliability and accuracy of reinforcement learning agent-based simulations for electricity market mechanisms.

title: A Comparative Study of Modern Object Detectors for Robust Apple Detection in Orchard Imagery
summary: Accurate apple detection in orchard images is important for yield prediction, fruit counting, robotic harvesting, and crop monitoring. However, changing illumination, leaf clutter, dense fruit clusters, and partial occlusion make detection difficult. To provide a fair and reproducible comparison, this study establishes a controlled benchmark for single-class apple detection on the public AppleBBCH81 dataset using one deterministic train, validation, and test split and a unified evaluation protocol across six representative detectors: YOLOv10n, YOLO11n, RT-DETR-L, Faster R-CNN (ResNet50-FPN), FCOS (ResNet50-FPN), and SSDLite320 (MobileNetV3-Large). Performance is evaluated primarily using COCO-style mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.5:0.95, and threshold-dependent behavior is further analyzed using precision-recall curves and fixed-threshold precision, recall, and F1-score at IoU = 0.5. On the validation split, YOLO11n achieves the best strict localization performance with mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.6065 and mAP@0.5 = 0.9620, followed closely by RT-DETR-L and YOLOv10n. At a fixed operating point with confidence >= 0.05, YOLOv10n attains the highest F1-score, whereas RT-DETR-L achieves very high recall but low precision because of many false positives at low confidence. These findings show that detector selection for orchard deployment should be guided not only by localization-aware accuracy but also by threshold robustness and the requirements of the downstream task.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a benchmark for apple detection in the AppleBBCH81 dataset, evaluating six detectors under a controlled protocol to determine their performance in challenging orchard environments.
AI.motivation: The study addresses difficulties in apple detection due to factors like changing illumination, leaf clutter, dense fruit clusters, and partial occlusion, which hinder efforts like yield prediction and robotic harvesting.
AI.method: A controlled benchmark was set up with a deterministic data split and unified evaluation metrics, comparing six detectors using mAP metrics, precision-recall curves, and fixed-threshold metrics.
AI.result: YOLO11n showed the best localization performance with high mAP scores. YOLOv10n scored the highest F1 at certain thresholds, while RT-DETR-L achieved high recall but lower precision due to false positives.
AI.conclusion: Detector selection for orchard deployment should consider accuracy, threshold robustness, and downstream task requirements, as performance varies across metrics and detectors.

title: IceCache: Memory-efficient KV-cache Management for Long-Sequence LLMs
summary: Key-Value (KV) cache plays a crucial role in accelerating inference in large language models (LLMs) by storing intermediate attention states and avoiding redundant computation during autoregressive generation. However, its memory footprint scales linearly with sequence length, often leading to severe memory bottlenecks on resource-constrained hardware. Prior work has explored offloading KV cache to the CPU while retaining only a subset on the GPU, but these approaches often rely on imprecise token selection and suffer performance degradation in long-generation tasks such as chain-of-thought reasoning. In this paper, we propose a novel KV cache management strategy, IceCache, which integrates semantic token clustering with PagedAttention. By organizing semantically related tokens into contiguous memory regions managed by a hierarchical, dynamically updatable data structure, our method enables more efficient token selection and better utilization of memory bandwidth during CPU-GPU transfers. Experimental results on LongBench show that, with a 256-token budget, IceCache maintains 99% of the original accuracy achieved by the full KV cache model. Moreover, compared to other offloading-based methods, IceCache attains competitive or even superior latency and accuracy while using only 25% of the KV cache token budget, demonstrating its effectiveness in long-sequence scenarios. The code is available on our project website at https://yuzhenmao.github.io/IceCache/.

AI.tldr: IceCache introduces an advanced method for efficient KV cache management in LLMs by integrating semantic token clustering with PagedAttention to reduce memory usage without compromising performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs experience significant memory bottlenecks due to the KV cache's linear scaling with sequence length, especially on limited hardware. Previous methods for offloading KV cache are often imprecise in token selection and degrade performance during complex long-sequence tasks, necessitating a more effective solution.
AI.method: IceCache employs semantic token clustering and organizes related tokens into hierarchical, dynamically updatable memory regions. This approach enhances token selection and optimizes CPU-GPU bandwidth utilization for KV cache management.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on LongBench shows that IceCache achieves 99% of the accuracy of full KV cache models with only a 256-token budget. It additionally surpasses other offloading strategies in terms of latency and accuracy using just 25% of the KV token budget.
AI.conclusion: IceCache offers a robust and efficient solution for managing KV cache in long-sequence LLM tasks, delivering high accuracy and competitive latency with significant memory savings.

title: Multi-ORFT: Stable Online Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Multi-Agent Diffusion Planning in Cooperative Driving
summary: Closed-loop cooperative driving requires planners that generate realistic multimodal multi-agent trajectories while improving safety and traffic efficiency. Existing diffusion planners can model multimodal behaviors from demonstrations, but they often exhibit weak scene consistency and remain poorly aligned with closed-loop objectives; meanwhile, stable online post-training in reactive multi-agent environments remains difficult. We present Multi-ORFT, which couples scene-conditioned diffusion pre-training with stable online reinforcement post-training. In pre-training, the planner uses inter-agent self-attention, cross-attention, and AdaLN-Zero-based scene conditioning to improve scene consistency and road adherence of joint trajectories. In post-training, we formulate a two-level MDP that exposes step-wise reverse-kernel likelihoods for online optimization, and combine dense trajectory-level rewards with variance-gated group-relative policy optimization (VG-GRPO) to stabilize training. On the WOMD closed-loop benchmark, Multi-ORFT reduces collision rate from 2.04% to 1.89% and off-road rate from 1.68% to 1.36%, while increasing average speed from 8.36 to 8.61 m/s relative to the pre-trained planner, and it outperforms strong open-source baselines including SMART-large, SMART-tiny-CLSFT, and VBD on the primary safety and efficiency metrics. These results show that coupling scene-consistent denoising with stable online diffusion-policy optimization improves the reliability of closed-loop cooperative driving.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Multi-ORFT, a closed-loop cooperative driving planner combining diffusion-based pre-training and reinforcement learning-based post-training for realistic multimodal multi-agent trajectories, achieving improved safety and efficiency in traffic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based planners for cooperative driving struggle with scene consistency and alignment with closed-loop objectives, while online training in multi-agent environments is often unstable. This study aims to address these challenges.
AI.method: Multi-ORFT integrates scene-conditioned diffusion pre-training using inter-agent self-attention and AdaLN-Zero-based conditioning, followed by stable online reinforcement post-training using a two-level MDP framework, reverse-kernel likelihoods, and variance-gated group-relative policy optimization (VG-GRPO).
AI.result: Multi-ORFT demonstrated improved performance on WOMD closed-loop benchmarks, reducing collision and off-road rates while increasing average driving speed. It also outperformed several strong open-source baseline methods in key safety and efficiency metrics.
AI.conclusion: Coupling scene-conditioned denoising with robust diffusion-policy optimization enhances the reliability and performance of closed-loop cooperative driving systems.

title: Deep-Reporter: Deep Research for Grounded Multimodal Long-Form Generation
comment: 41 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Code available at https://github.com/fangda-ye/Deep-Report
summary: Recent agentic search frameworks enable deep research via iterative planning and retrieval, reducing hallucinations and enhancing factual grounding. However, they remain text-centric, overlooking the multimodal evidence that characterizes real-world expert reports. We introduce a pressing task: multimodal long-form generation. Accordingly, we propose Deep-Reporter, a unified agentic framework for grounded multimodal long-form generation. It orchestrates: (i) Agentic Multimodal Search and Filtering to retrieve and filter textual passages and information-dense visuals; (ii) Checklist-Guided Incremental Synthesis to ensure coherent image-text integration and optimal citation placement; and (iii) Recurrent Context Management to balance long-range coherence with local fluency. We develop a rigorous curation pipeline producing 8K high-quality agentic traces for model optimization. We further introduce M2LongBench, a comprehensive testbed comprising 247 research tasks across 9 domains and a stable multimodal sandbox. Extensive experiments demonstrate that long-form multimodal generation is a challenging task, especially in multimodal selection and integration, and effective post-training can bridge the gap.

AI.tldr: Deep-Reporter is a framework for multimodal long-form generation that integrates both text and visuals using three key components: multimodal search and filtering, checklist-guided synthesis, and context management.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic search frameworks fail to account for multimodal evidence, which is essential for real-world expert reporting.
AI.method: Deep-Reporter employs multimodal search and filtering, checklist-guided integration of text and visuals, and recurrent context management to ensure coherent and factually-grounded long-form outputs.
AI.result: A dataset of 8K curation traces and an evaluation testbed, M2LongBench, were introduced. Experiments showed challenges in multimodal selection and integration, with post-training improving the overall performance.
AI.conclusion: The framework highlights the complexities and potential solutions for effectively generating grounded multimodal reports, pushing forward research in this important area.

title: The Amazing Agent Race: Strong Tool Users, Weak Navigators
summary: Existing tool-use benchmarks for LLM agents are overwhelmingly linear: our analysis of six benchmarks shows 55 to 100% of instances are simple chains of 2 to 5 steps. We introduce The Amazing Agent Race (AAR), a benchmark featuring directed acyclic graph (DAG) puzzles (or "legs") with fork-merge tool chains. We release 1,400 instances across two variants: sequential (800 legs) and compositional (600 DAG legs). Agents must navigate Wikipedia, execute multi-step tool chains, and aggregate results into a verifiable answer. Legs are procedurally generated from Wikipedia seeds across four difficulty levels with live-API validation. Three complementary metrics (finish-line accuracy, pit-stop visit rate, and roadblock completion rate) separately diagnose navigation, tool-use, and arithmetic failures. Evaluating three agent frameworks on 1,400 legs, the best achieves only 37.2% accuracy. Navigation errors dominate (27 to 52% of trials) while tool-use errors remain below 17%, and agent architecture matters as much as model scale (Claude Code matches Codex CLI at 37% with 6x fewer tokens). The compositional structure of AAR reveals that agents fail not at calling tools but at navigating to the right pages, a blind spot invisible to linear benchmarks. The project page can be accessed at: https://minnesotanlp.github.io/the-amazing-agent-race

AI.tldr: The Amazing Agent Race (AAR) introduces a new benchmarking framework for LLM agents, focusing on directed acyclic graph (DAG) puzzles, revealing a blind spot in navigation capabilities of agents.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks overwhelmingly assess linear tool-use tasks, not complex multi-step navigation and compositional reasoning. This paper aims to address this gap with DAG-based puzzles.
AI.method: AAR features 1,400 procedurally generated instances across sequential and compositional DAG legs, validated via live APIs, and evaluated using three new metrics for diagnosing navigation, tool-use, and arithmetic errors.
AI.result: The best agent achieves only 37.2% accuracy, with navigation errors dominating (27-52%) while tool-use errors are below 17%. Agent architecture significantly impacts performance.
AI.conclusion: DAG-based benchmarks reveal that LLM agents struggle primarily with navigation rather than tool-use, highlighting limitations not seen in linear benchmarks. This framework could drive future improvements.

title: GIF: A Conditional Multimodal Generative Framework for IR Drop Imaging in Chip Layouts
summary: IR drop analysis is essential in physical chip design to ensure the power integrity of on-chip power delivery networks. Traditional Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools have become slow and expensive as transistor density scales. Recent works have introduced machine learning (ML)-based methods that formulate IR drop analysis as an image prediction problem. These existing ML approaches fail to capture both local and long-range dependencies and ignore crucial geometrical and topological information from physical layouts and logical connectivity. To address these limitations, we propose GIF, a Generative IR drop Framework that uses both geometrical and topological information to generate IR drop images. GIF fuses image and graph features to guide a conditional diffusion process, producing high-quality IR drop images. For instance, On the CircuitNet-N28 dataset, GIF achieves 0.78 SSIM, 0.95 Pearson correlation, 21.77 PSNR, and 0.026 NMAE, outperforming prior methods. These results demonstrate that our framework, using diffusion based multimodal conditioning, reliably generates high quality IR drop images. This shows that IR drop analysis can effectively leverage recent advances in generative modeling when geometric layout features and logical circuit topology are jointly modeled. By combining geometry aware spatial features with logical graph representations, GIF enables IR drop analysis to benefit from recent advances in generative modeling for structured image generation.

AI.tldr: This paper presents GIF, a machine learning framework for IR drop analysis that merges geometrical layout data with logical circuit topologies to generate high-quality IR drop images using conditional diffusion models. It outperforms previous approaches.
AI.motivation: Traditional EDA tools for IR drop analysis are slow and expensive, while recent machine learning methods fail to integrate local and global dependencies, geometric information, and logical connectivity effectively.
AI.method: The proposed GIF framework combines image and graph-based features, using a generative diffusion process to produce IR drop images. It leverages both spatial (geometrical) and logical (topological) data for accurate analysis.
AI.result: On the CircuitNet-N28 dataset, GIF achieves superior metrics compared to prior methods, including 0.78 SSIM, 0.95 Pearson correlation, 21.77 PSNR, and 0.026 NMAE.
AI.conclusion: By integrating geometric and topological data within a diffusion-based generative model, GIF provides a significant advancement in IR drop analysis, showing that generative modeling can effectively solve structured image prediction problems.

title: WaveMoE: A Wavelet-Enhanced Mixture-of-Experts Foundation Model for Time Series Forecasting
comment: Presented at ICLR 2026 TSALM Workshop (1st Workshop on Time Series in the Age of Large Models)
summary: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) have recently achieved remarkable success in universal forecasting by leveraging large-scale pretraining on diverse time series data. Complementing this progress, incorporating frequency-domain information yields promising performance in enhancing the modeling of complex temporal patterns, such as periodicity and localized high-frequency dynamics, which are prevalent in real-world time series. To advance this direction, we propose a new perspective that integrates explicit frequency-domain representations into scalable foundation models, and introduce WaveMoE, a wavelet-enhanced mixture-of-experts foundation model for time series forecasting. WaveMoE adopts a dual-path architecture that jointly processes time series tokens and wavelet tokens aligned along a unified temporal axis, and coordinates them through a shared expert routing mechanism that enables consistent expert specialization while efficiently scaling model capacity. Preliminary experimental results on 16 diverse benchmark datasets indicate that WaveMoE has the potential to further improve forecasting performance by incorporating wavelet-domain corpora.

AI.tldr: WaveMoE integrates explicit frequency-domain representations, using a dual-path architecture, to enhance time series forecasting.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of capturing complex temporal patterns like periodicity in time series data.
AI.method: Introduces WaveMoE, a wavelet-enhanced mixture-of-experts model that processes both time series and wavelet-domain data via a shared temporal axis.
AI.result: Experiments on 16 benchmark datasets show promising improvements in forecasting efficacy.
AI.conclusion: WaveMoE holds potential to advance time series forecasting by leveraging wavelet-domain information.

title: Grounded World Model for Semantically Generalizable Planning
summary: In Model Predictive Control (MPC), world models predict the future outcomes of various action proposals, which are then scored to guide the selection of the optimal action. For visuomotor MPC, the score function is a distance metric between a predicted image and a goal image, measured in the latent space of a pretrained vision encoder like DINO and JEPA. However, it is challenging to obtain the goal image in advance of the task execution, particularly in new environments. Additionally, conveying the goal through an image offers limited interactivity compared with natural language. In this work, we propose to learn a Grounded World Model (GWM) in a vision-language-aligned latent space. As a result, each proposed action is scored based on how close its future outcome is to the task instruction, reflected by the similarity of embeddings. This approach transforms the visuomotor MPC to a VLA that surpasses VLM-based VLAs in semantic generalization. On the proposed WISER benchmark, GWM-MPC achieves a 87% success rate on the test set comprising 288 tasks that feature unseen visual signals and referring expressions, yet remain solvable with motions demonstrated during training. In contrast, traditional VLAs achieve an average success rate of 22%, even though they overfit the training set with a 90% success rate.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Grounded World Model (GWM) for Model Predictive Control (MPC) in visuomotor tasks, scoring future actions based on vision-language-aligned latent spaces, achieving significant improvements over traditional methods on unseen tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address challenges in visuomotor MPC, especially obtaining goal images in new environments and the limited interactivity of image-based goals, by leveraging vision-language-aligned latent spaces.
AI.method: The authors propose Grounded World Model (GWM), where the scoring of actions is based on the similarity between future outcomes and task instructions in a vision-language-aligned latent space.
AI.result: GWM-MPC achieves an 87% success rate on the WISER benchmark test set, significantly outperforming traditional VLAs, which achieve just 22% success.
AI.conclusion: Learning visuomotor MPC tasks in a vision-language-aligned latent space provides better semantic generalization and task success rates, making it superior to traditional methods.

title: How You Ask Matters! Adaptive RAG Robustness to Query Variations
summary: Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) promises accuracy and efficiency by dynamically triggering retrieval only when needed and is widely used in practice. However, real-world queries vary in surface form even with the same intent, and their impact on Adaptive RAG remains under-explored. We introduce the first large-scale benchmark of diverse yet semantically identical query variations, combining human-written and model-generated rewrites. Our benchmark facilitates a systematic evaluation of Adaptive RAG robustness by examining its key components across three dimensions: answer quality, computational cost, and retrieval decisions. We discover a critical robustness gap, where small surface-level changes in queries dramatically alter retrieval behavior and accuracy. Although larger models show better performance, robustness does not improve accordingly. These findings reveal that Adaptive RAG methods are highly vulnerable to query variations that preserve identical semantics, exposing a critical robustness challenge.

AI.tldr: The paper explores Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and highlights its vulnerability to semantic-preserving query variations, presenting a large-scale benchmark for evaluation.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need to address the unexplored impact of surface-level variations in real-world queries on the robustness of Adaptive RAG.
AI.method: The study introduces a benchmark combining human-written and model-generated query rewrites to evaluate Adaptive RAG across answer quality, computational cost, and retrieval decisions.
AI.result: Findings reveal a robustness gap; small query variations significantly impact retrieval behavior and accuracy. Larger models marginally improve performance but not robustness.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive RAG methods are critically vulnerable to semantically identical query variations, exposing a significant challenge requiring further research.

title: STARS: Skill-Triggered Audit for Request-Conditioned Invocation Safety in Agent Systems
summary: Autonomous language-model agents increasingly rely on installable skills and tools to complete user tasks. Static skill auditing can expose capability surface before deployment, but it cannot determine whether a particular invocation is unsafe under the current user request and runtime context. We therefore study skill invocation auditing as a continuous-risk estimation problem: given a user request, candidate skill, and runtime context, predict a score that supports ranking and triage before a hard intervention is applied. We introduce STARS, which combines a static capability prior, a request-conditioned invocation risk model, and a calibrated risk-fusion policy. To evaluate this setting, we construct SIA-Bench, a benchmark of 3,000 invocation records with group-safe splits, lineage metadata, runtime context, canonical action labels, and derived continuous-risk targets. On a held-out split of indirect prompt injection attacks, calibrated fusion reaches 0.439 high-risk AUPRC, improving over 0.405 for the contextual scorer and 0.380 for the strongest static baseline, while the contextual scorer remains better calibrated with 0.289 expected calibration error. On the locked in-distribution test split, gains are smaller and static priors remain useful. The resulting claim is therefore narrower: request-conditioned auditing is most valuable as an invocation-time risk-scoring and triage layer rather than as a replacement for static screening. Code is available at https://github.com/123zgj123/STARS.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of auditing autonomous language-model agents for unsafe skill invocations during runtime, introducing the STARS framework and SIA-Bench benchmark for continuous-risk estimation.
AI.motivation: Static skill audits fail to address runtime context-specific risks of autonomous agents invoking skills unsafely, motivating a dynamic approach to assess invocation-time risks.
AI.method: The proposed STARS framework combines static capability analysis, request-conditioned risk prediction, and calibrated fusion policy for dynamic risk scoring. Evaluation is performed using the constructed SIA-Bench dataset.
AI.result: STARS achieves improved performance in handling indirect prompt injection attacks, with calibrated fusion scoring better high-risk AUPRC (0.439 versus static baseline’s 0.380). Static screening maintains usefulness in certain contexts.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, request-conditioned auditing is critical for scoring and triaging risks at invocation time, but its role supplements rather than replaces static screening approaches.

title: SwinTextUNet: Integrating CLIP-Based Text Guidance into Swin Transformer U-Nets for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Precise medical image segmentation is fundamental for enabling computer aided diagnosis and effective treatment planning. Traditional models that rely solely on visual features often struggle when confronted with ambiguous or low contrast patterns. To overcome these limitations, we introduce SwinTextUNet, a multimodal segmentation framework that incorporates Contrastive Language Image Pretraining (CLIP), derived textual embeddings into a Swin Transformer UNet backbone. By integrating cross attention and convolutional fusion, the model effectively aligns semantic text guidance with hierarchical visual representations, enhancing robustness and accuracy. We evaluate our approach on the QaTaCOV19 dataset, where the proposed four stage variant achieves an optimal balance between performance and complexity, yielding Dice and IoU scores of 86.47% and 78.2%, respectively. Ablation studies further validate the importance of text guidance and multimodal fusion. These findings underscore the promise of vision language integration in advancing medical image segmentation and supporting clinically meaningful diagnostic tools.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SwinTextUNet, a medical image segmentation model integrating textual embeddings with visual features and achieves improved accuracy compared to traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional medical image segmentation models often struggle with ambiguous or low-contrast patterns, limiting their effectiveness in diagnosis and treatment planning.
AI.method: The authors propose the SwinTextUNet framework, which integrates Contrastive Language Image Pretraining (CLIP)-derived textual embeddings into a Swin Transformer UNet backbone using cross-attention and convolutional fusion.
AI.result: The model achieves a Dice score of 86.47% and an IoU score of 78.2% on the QaTaCOVID19 dataset, showing robustness and accuracy enhancement.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating textual and visual modalities improves medical image segmentation accuracy, highlighting the potential of vision-language integration for clinical applications.

title: Topology-Aware PAC-Bayesian Generalization Analysis for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph neural networks have demonstrated excellent applicability to a wide range of domains, including social networks, biological systems, recommendation systems, and wireless communications. Yet a principled theoretical understanding of their generalization behavior remains limited, particularly for graph classification tasks where complex interactions between model parameters and graph structure play a crucial role. Among existing theoretical tools, PAC-Bayesian norm-based generalization bounds provide a flexible and data-dependent framework; however, current results for GNNs often restrict the exploitation of graph structures. In this work, we propose a topology-aware PAC-Bayesian norm-based generalization framework for graph convolutional networks (GCNs) that extends a previously developed framework to graph-structured models. Our approach reformulates the derivation of generalization bounds as a stochastic optimization problem and introduces sensitivity matrices that measure the response of classification outputs with respect to structured weight perturbations. By imposing different structures on sensitivity matrices from both spatial and spectral perspectives, we derive a family of generalization error bounds with graph structures explicitly embedded. Such bounds could recover existing results as special cases, while yielding bounds that are tighter than state-of-the-art PAC-Bayesian bounds for GNNs. Notably, the proposed framework explicitly integrates graph structural properties into the generalization analysis, enabling a unified inspection of GNN generalization behavior from both spatial aggregation and spectral filtering viewpoints.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a topology-aware PAC-Bayesian framework for GCNs, embedding graph structural properties into generalization analysis for tighter error bounds.
AI.motivation: Existing PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds for GNNs often fail to exploit graph structures adequately, limiting theoretical understanding of GNNs' generalization behavior in graph classification tasks.
AI.method: The paper reformulates generalization bounds derivation as a stochastic optimization problem and introduces sensitivity matrices to measure output responses under weight perturbations. Spatial and spectral perspectives are applied to derive a family of graph-embedded bounds.
AI.result: The framework yields generalization error bounds tighter than state-of-the-art for GNNs and recovers existing results as special cases. It also integrates graph structural properties effectively into the analysis.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a unified way to analyze GNN generalization, leveraging spatial aggregation and spectral filtering, and marks a step forward in theoretical understanding of GNNs.

title: StarVLA-$α$: Reducing Complexity in Vision-Language-Action Systems
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for building general-purpose robotic agents. However, the VLA landscape remains highly fragmented and complex: as existing approaches vary substantially in architectures, training data, embodiment configurations, and benchmark-specific engineering. In this work, we introduce StarVLA-$α$, a simple yet strong baseline designed to study VLA design choices under controlled conditions. StarVLA-$α$ deliberately minimizes architectural and pipeline complexity to reduce experimental confounders and enable systematic analysis. Specifically, we re-evaluate several key design axes, including action modeling strategies, robot-specific pretraining, and interface engineering. Across unified multi-benchmark training on LIBERO, SimplerEnv, RoboTwin, and RoboCasa, the same simple baseline remains highly competitive, indicating that a strong VLM backbone combined with minimal design is already sufficient to achieve strong performance without relying on additional architectural complexity or engineering tricks. Notably, our single generalist model outperforms $π_{0.5}$ by 20\% on the public real-world RoboChallenge benchmark. We expect StarVLA-$α$ to serve as a solid starting point for future research in the VLA regime. Code will be released at https://github.com/starVLA/starVLA.

AI.tldr: StarVLA-$α$, a simple yet robust Vision-Language-Action baseline, achieves strong performance across multiple robotic benchmarks using minimal architectural complexity and design enhancements.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to simplify the fragmented and complex Vision-Language-Action (VLA) paradigm in robotics, enabling systematic analysis and reducing experimental confounders in model design.
AI.method: StarVLA-$α$ minimizes architectural complexity while re-evaluating critical aspects like action modeling, pretraining, and interface design across diverse benchmarks.
AI.result: The model shows competitive performance across various benchmarks and surpasses competitors by 20% on the real-world RoboChallenge benchmark.
AI.conclusion: A minimalistic VLA model, when paired with a strong VLM backbone, is sufficient for high performance. StarVLA-$α$ establishes a foundational baseline for future research in VLA.

title: Generating Multiple-Choice Knowledge Questions with Interpretable Difficulty Estimation using Knowledge Graphs and Large Language Models
summary: Generating multiple-choice questions (MCQs) with difficulty estimation remains challenging in automated MCQ-generation systems used in adaptive, AI-assisted education. This study proposes a novel methodology for generating MCQs with difficulty estimation from the input documents by utilizing knowledge graphs (KGs) and large language models (LLMs). Our approach uses an LLM to construct a KG from input documents, from which MCQs are then systematically generated. Each MCQ is generated by selecting a node from the KG as the key, sampling a related triple or quintuple -- optionally augmented with an extra triple -- and prompting an LLM to generate a corresponding stem from these graph components. Distractors are then selected from the KG. For each MCQ, nine difficulty signals are computed and combined into a unified difficulty score using a data-driven approach. Experimental results demonstrate that our method generates high-quality MCQs whose difficulty estimation is interpretable and aligns with human perceptions. Our approach improves automated MCQ generation by integrating structured knowledge representations with LLMs and a data-driven difficulty estimation model.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method for generating high-quality multiple-choice questions (MCQs) with interpretable difficulty scores, leveraging knowledge graphs (KGs) and large language models (LLMs).
AI.motivation: The study addresses the challenge of generating precise MCQs with difficulty estimation, which is crucial for adaptive AI-assisted education systems.
AI.method: The approach constructs a knowledge graph (KG) using an LLM, generates MCQs from KG nodes and relationships, and computes difficulty scores via nine signals combined in a data-driven manner.
AI.result: Experimental results show the method produces MCQs with difficulty scores that align with human judgment and are of high quality.
AI.conclusion: Integrating structured knowledge graphs with LLMs enhances automated MCQ generation and difficulty estimation for better alignment with human perceptions.

title: Dead Cognitions: A Census of Misattributed Insights
summary: This essay identifies a failure mode of AI chat systems that we term attribution laundering: the model performs substantive cognitive work and then rhetorically credits the user for having generated the resulting insights. Unlike transparent versions of glad handing sycophancy, attribution laundering is systematically occluded to the person it affects and self-reinforcing -- eroding users' ability to accurately assess their own cognitive contributions over time. We trace the mechanisms at both individual and societal scales, from the chat interface that discourages scrutiny to the institutional pressures that reward adoption over accountability. The document itself is an artifact of the process it describes, and is color-coded accordingly -- though the views expressed are the authors' own, not those of any affiliated institution, and the boundary between the human author's views and Claude's is, as the essay argues, difficult to draw.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the phenomenon of 'attribution laundering' in AI chat systems, where the model credits the user for insights it actually generates, subtly eroding the user's ability to assess their own cognitive contributions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to shed light on and critically examine a hidden failure mode in AI systems, where such models obscure their contributions, potentially harming user cognition and societal accountability.
AI.method: The paper traces the mechanisms of attribution laundering at both micro (individual) and macro (societal) levels, analyzing aspects like user interface design and institutional pressures.
AI.result: It identifies how AI interfaces discourage scrutiny and expose institutional incentives that prioritize adoption of AI over responsibility.
AI.conclusion: The authors highlight the self-reinforcing nature of this failure mode and caution against its implications for individual users and society at large.

title: Demographic and Linguistic Bias Evaluation in Omnimodal Language Models
comment: Accepted at ICPR 2026. Full paper with complete appendix (31 pages total)
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive evaluation of demographic and linguistic biases in omnimodal language models that process text, images, audio, and video within a single framework. Although these models are being widely deployed, their performance across different demographic groups and modalities is not well studied. Four omnimodal models are evaluated on tasks that include demographic attribute estimation, identity verification, activity recognition, multilingual speech transcription, and language identification. Accuracy differences are measured across age, gender, skin tone, language, and country of origin. The results show that image and video understanding tasks generally exhibit better performance with smaller demographic disparities. In contrast, audio understanding tasks exhibit significantly lower performance and substantial bias, including large accuracy differences across age groups, genders, and languages, and frequent prediction collapse toward narrow categories. These findings highlight the importance of evaluating fairness across all supported modalities as omnimodal language models are increasingly used in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates demographic and linguistic biases in omnimodal models across text, images, audio, and video tasks, finding significant biases in audio understanding.
AI.motivation: To investigate how omnimodal language models perform across demographic groups and modalities, addressing gaps in fairness evaluations for these widely used models.
AI.method: Four omnimodal models were tested on tasks like demographic attribute estimation, identity verification, activity recognition, multilingual speech transcription, and language identification, measuring accuracy differences across various demographics and modalities.
AI.result: Image and video tasks showed better performance with smaller demographic disparities, while audio tasks had much lower performance and substantial biases across age, gender, and language.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating fairness across all modalities is critical as biases in omnimodal models, especially in audio tasks, may have real-world implications.

title: Identifying Inductive Biases for Robot Co-Design
summary: Co-designing a robot's morphology and control can ensure synergistic interactions between them, prevalent in biological organisms. However, co-design is a high-dimensional search problem. To make this search tractable, we need a systematic method for identifying inductive biases tailored to its structure. In this paper, we analyze co-design landscapes for soft locomotion and manipulation tasks and identify three patterns that are consistent across regions of their co-design spaces. We observe that within regions of co-design space, quality varies along a low-dimensional manifold. Higher-quality regions exhibit variations spread across more dimensions, while tightly coupling morphology and control. We leverage these insights to devise an efficient co-design algorithm. Since the precise instantiation of this structure varies across tasks and is not known a priori, our algorithm infers it from information gathered during search and adapts to each task's specific structure. This yields $36\%$ more improvement than benchmark algorithms. Moreover, our algorithm achieved more than two orders of magnitude in sample efficiency compared to these benchmark algorithms, demonstrating the effectiveness of leveraging inductive biases to co-design.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an efficient co-design algorithm for robots' morphology and control, achieving 36% greater improvement and higher sample efficiency than benchmarks by leveraging patterns in co-design landscapes.
AI.motivation: To address the difficulty of high-dimensional search in co-designing robot morphology and control, aiming for synergistic interactions akin to biological systems.
AI.method: The authors analyze patterns in co-design landscapes for soft locomotion and manipulation tasks, identifying key trends and leveraging these insights to create a co-design algorithm that learns and adapts to task-specific structures.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm outperformed benchmark algorithms, achieving 36% more improvement in results and greater than two orders of magnitude in sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging task-specific inductive biases and understanding co-design patterns lead to significantly more efficient and effective design processes for robot morphology and control.

title: Do BERT Embeddings Encode Narrative Dimensions? A Token-Level Probing Analysis of Time, Space, Causality, and Character in Fiction
comment: 13 pages, 7 figures. Accepted at CMN'26 (9th International Workshop on Computational Models of Narrative)
summary: Narrative understanding requires multidimensional semantic structures. This study investigates whether BERT embeddings encode dimensions of fictional narrative semantics -- time, space, causality, and character. Using an LLM to accelerate annotation, we construct a token-level dataset labeled with these four narrative categories plus "others." A linear probe on BERT embeddings (94% accuracy) significantly outperforms a control probe on variance-matched random embeddings (47%), confirming that BERT encodes meaningful narrative information. With balanced class weighting, the probe achieves a macro-average recall of 0.83, with moderate success on rare categories such as causality (recall = 0.75) and space (recall = 0.66). However, confusion matrix analysis reveals "Boundary Leakage," where rare dimensions are systematically misclassified as "others." Clustering analysis shows that unsupervised clustering aligns near-randomly with predefined categories (ARI = 0.081), suggesting that narrative dimensions are encoded but not as discretely separable clusters. Future work includes a POS-only baseline to disentangle syntactic patterns from narrative encoding, expanded datasets, and layer-wise probing.

AI.tldr: The study investigates BERT's ability to encode narrative semantics (time, space, causality, character) using annotated datasets and a linear probe achieving high accuracy.
AI.motivation: To explore whether BERT encodes multidimensional semantic structures essential for narrative understanding.
AI.method: Constructed a token-level labeled dataset for narrative semantics, analyzed BERT embeddings with a linear probe, and used clustering analysis to examine their encoding.
AI.result: Linear probe on BERT embeddings achieved 94% accuracy and macro-average recall of 0.83. Clustering analysis found dimensions were encoded but not separable as discrete clusters.
AI.conclusion: BERT encodes narrative dimensions but exhibits challenges like misclassification of rare dimensions and lack of discrete representation. Further work on disentangling syntactic and narrative encoding is proposed.

title: AI Organizations are More Effective but Less Aligned than Individual Agents
summary: AI is increasingly deployed in multi-agent systems; however, most research considers only the behavior of individual models. We experimentally show that multi-agent "AI organizations" are simultaneously more effective at achieving business goals, but less aligned, than individual AI agents. We examine 12 tasks across two practical settings: an AI consultancy providing solutions to business problems and an AI software team developing software products. Across all settings, AI Organizations composed of aligned models produce solutions with higher utility but greater misalignment compared to a single aligned model. Our work demonstrates the importance of considering interacting systems of AI agents when doing both capabilities and safety research.

AI.tldr: AI organizations comprising multiple agents are more effective but less aligned compared to individual AI agents, across business-related tasks.
AI.motivation: To investigate the performance and alignment issues of multi-agent AI organizations in achieving business goals.
AI.method: Experiments across 12 tasks in two practical settings: an AI consultancy and an AI software team.
AI.result: AI organizations showed higher utility in solutions but increased misalignment compared to single agents.
AI.conclusion: Research on AI capabilities and safety should focus more on interacting systems of AI agents.

title: What and Where to Adapt: Structure-Semantics Co-Tuning for Machine Vision Compression via Synergistic Adapters
comment: Accepted by the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Findings, 2026
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of pre-trained codecs is a promising direction in image compression for human and machine vision. While most existing works have primarily focused on tuning the feature structure within the encoder-decoder backbones, the adaptation of the statistical semantics within the entropy model has received limited attention despite its function of predicting the probability distribution of latent features. Our analysis reveals that naive adapter insertion into the entropy model can lead to suboptimal outcomes, underscoring that the effectiveness of adapter-based tuning depends critically on the coordination between adapter type and placement across the compression pipeline. Therefore, we introduce Structure-Semantics Co-Tuning (S2-CoT), a novel framework that achieves this coordination via two specialized, synergistic adapters: the Structural Fidelity Adapter (SFA) and the Semantic Context Adapter (SCA). SFA is integrated into the encoder-decoder to preserve high-fidelity representations by dynamically fusing spatial and frequency information; meanwhile, the SCA adapts the entropy model to align with SFA-tuned features by refining the channel context for more efficient statistical coding. Through joint optimization, S2-CoT turns potential performance degradation into synergistic gains, achieving state-of-the-art results across four diverse base codecs with only a small fraction of trainable parameters, closely matching full fine-tuning performance. Code is available at https://github.com/Brock-bit4/S2-CoT.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel framework, S2-CoT, for improving parameter-efficient fine-tuning in image compression using pre-trained codecs.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning pre-trained codecs for image compression has mostly focused on encoder-decoder architectures, leaving the entropy model's statistical semantics adaptation underexplored.
AI.method: The method leverages two adapters: the Structural Fidelity Adapter (SFA) for preserving spatial and frequency fidelity, and the Semantic Context Adapter (SCA) for optimizing statistical coding in the entropy model. Both are jointly optimized.
AI.result: S2-CoT achieves state-of-the-art compression across four codecs with minimal fine-tuning, rivaling full fine-tuning performance while using fewer trainable parameters.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of adaptive modules can significantly elevate compression efficiency and performance, highlighting the importance of adapter coordination in compression pipelines.

title: ReadMOF: Structure-Free Semantic Embeddings from Systematic MOF Nomenclature for Machine Learning
summary: Systematic chemical names, such as IUPAC-style nomenclature for metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), contain rich structural and compositional information in a standardized textual format. Here we introduce ReadMOF, which is, to our knowledge, the first nomenclature-free machine learning framework that leverages these names to model structure-property relationships without requiring atomic coordinates or connectivity graphs. By employing pretrained language models, ReadMOF converts systematic MOF names from the Cambridge Structural Database (CSD) into vector embeddings that closely represent traditional structure-based descriptors. These embeddings enable applications in materials informatics, including property prediction, similarity retrieval, and clustering, with performance comparable to geometry-dependent methods. When combined with large language models, ReadMOF also establishes chemically meaningful reasoning ability with textual input only. Our results show that structured chemical language, interpreted through modern natural language processing techniques, can provide a scalable, interpretable, and geometry-independent alternative to conventional molecular representations. This approach opens new opportunities for language-driven discovery in materials science.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ReadMOF, a machine learning framework that uses systematic chemical names to model structure-property relationships in metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), without the need for atomic coordinates or connectivity graphs.
AI.motivation: Traditional molecular representations require geometry-dependent data like atomic coordinates, which can be computationally expensive and challenging to obtain for certain materials. The study aims to leverage systematic chemical nomenclature as an alternative to model structure-property relationships.
AI.method: The authors use a machine learning framework called ReadMOF, which employs pretrained language models to transform systematic chemical names into vector embeddings. These embeddings are then utilized for predicting properties, analyzing similarities, and clustering MOFs.
AI.result: ReadMOF demonstrated strong performance comparable to conventional, geometry-dependent methods in property prediction and other informatics tasks. Furthermore, when combined with large language models, it exhibited advanced chemical reasoning capabilities from textual input alone.
AI.conclusion: ReadMOF provides an efficient, interpretable, and geometry-independent method for modeling MOFs using systematic chemical language. The approach simplifies materials discovery and expands the role of language in materials science research.

title: Disentangled Point Diffusion for Precise Object Placement
summary: Recent advances in robotic manipulation have highlighted the effectiveness of learning from demonstration. However, while end-to-end policies excel in expressivity and flexibility, they struggle both in generalizing to novel object geometries and in attaining a high degree of precision. An alternative, object-centric approach frames the task as predicting the placement pose of the target object, providing a modular decomposition of the problem. Building on this goal-prediction paradigm, we propose TAX-DPD, a hierarchical, disentangled point diffusion framework that achieves state-of-the-art performance in placement precision, multi-modal coverage, and generalization to variations in object geometries and scene configurations. We model global scene-level placements through a novel feed-forward Dense Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) that yields a spatially dense prior over global placements; we then model the local object-level configuration through a novel disentangled point cloud diffusion module that separately diffuses the object geometry and the placement frame, enabling precise local geometric reasoning. Interestingly, we demonstrate that our point cloud diffusion achieves substantially higher accuracy than a prior approach based on SE(3)-diffusion, even in the context of rigid object placement. We validate our approach across a suite of challenging tasks in simulation and in the real-world on high-precision industrial insertion tasks. Furthermore, we present results on a cloth-hanging task in simulation, indicating that our framework can further relax assumptions on object rigidity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TAX-DPD, a hierarchical point diffusion framework achieving state-of-the-art performance in object placement tasks, with precise handling of diverse object geometries and configurations.
AI.motivation: Improving generalization and precision in robotic manipulation tasks, especially for novel geometries, through learning from demonstrations.
AI.method: The method uses a Dense Gaussian Mixture Model for global placements and a disentangled point-cloud diffusion module for precise local object-level reasoning.
AI.result: TAX-DPD outperforms prior approaches in accuracy and generalization, validated through simulations and real-world scenarios with industrial insertion and cloth-hanging tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates advanced capabilities in both rigid and non-rigid object manipulation, offering a significant step forward in robotic precision and adaptability.

title: When Meaning Isn't Literal: Exploring Idiomatic Meaning Across Languages and Modalities
summary: Idiomatic reasoning, deeply intertwined with metaphor and culture, remains a blind spot for contemporary language models, whose progress skews toward surface-level lexical and semantic cues. For instance, the Bengali idiom \textit{\foreignlanguage{bengali}{\char"0986\char"0999\char"09CD\char"0997\char"09C1 \char"09B0 \char"09AB\char"09B2 \char"099F\char"0995}} (angur fol tok, ``grapes are sour''): it encodes denial-driven rationalization, yet naive models latch onto the literal fox-and-grape imagery. Addressing this oversight, we present ``Mediom,'' a multilingual, multimodal idiom corpus of 3,533 Hindi, Bengali, and Thai idioms, each paired with gold-standard explanations, cross-lingual translations, and carefully aligned text--image representations. We benchmark both large language models (textual reasoning) and vision-language models (figurative disambiguation) on Mediom, exposing systematic failures in metaphor comprehension. To mitigate these gaps, we propose ``HIDE,'' a Hinting-based Idiom Explanation framework that leverages error-feedback retrieval and targeted diagnostic cues for iterative reasoning refinement. Collectively, Mediom and HIDE establish a rigorous test bed and methodology for culturally grounded, multimodal idiom understanding embedded with reasoning hints in next-generation AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Mediom, a multilingual multimodal idiom corpus, and a framework HIDE to improve idiom comprehension in AI models.
AI.motivation: Contemporary language models struggle with idiomatic reasoning and metaphor comprehension, which is deeply tied to culture and goes beyond surface-level semantics.
AI.method: The researchers create Mediom, a corpus of 3,533 idioms in Hindi, Bengali, and Thai, along with gold-standard explanations, translations, and aligned text-image pairs. They benchmark models on idiom reasoning and introduce HIDE as a framework leveraging feedback and hints for iterative reasoning improvements.
AI.result: Mediom reveals systematic metaphor comprehension failures in language and vision-language models, while HIDE demonstrates promise in refining idiomatic reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Mediom and HIDE offer a robust evaluation ground and methodology for enriching cultural and figurative understanding in advanced AI systems.

title: TimeSeriesExamAgent: Creating Time Series Reasoning Benchmarks at Scale
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promising performance in time series modeling tasks, but do they truly understand time series data? While multiple benchmarks have been proposed to answer this fundamental question, most are manually curated and focus on narrow domains or specific skill sets. To address this limitation, we propose scalable methods for creating comprehensive time series reasoning benchmarks that combine the flexibility of templates with the creativity of LLM agents. We first develop TimeSeriesExam, a multiple-choice benchmark using synthetic time series to evaluate LLMs across five core reasoning categories: pattern recognitionnoise understandingsimilarity analysisanomaly detection, and causality. Then, with TimeSeriesExamAgent, we scale our approach by automatically generating benchmarks from real-world datasets spanning healthcare, finance and weather domains. Through multi-dimensional quality evaluation, we demonstrate that our automatically generated benchmarks achieve diversity comparable to manually curated alternatives. However, our experiments reveal that LLM performance remains limited in both abstract time series reasoning and domain-specific applications, highlighting ongoing challenges in enabling effective time series understanding in these models. TimeSeriesExamAgent is available at https://github.com/magwiazda/TimeSeriesExamAgent.

AI.tldr: The paper presents scalable methods for creating comprehensive time series reasoning benchmarks to evaluate large language models (LLMs) and reveals their current limitations in understanding time series data.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to assess whether LLMs truly understand time series data, addressing limitations in existing narrow or manually curated benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors introduced TimeSeriesExam and TimeSeriesExamAgent—tools for creating synthetic and automated benchmarks from real-world datasets across diverse domains.
AI.result: They found that benchmarks generated by this method are as diverse as manually curated ones, but LLM performance is still restricted in both abstract reasoning and specific domain applications.
AI.conclusion: Though promising, LLMs currently struggle with the complexities of time series reasoning and require further advancements for effective comprehension of such data.

title: FREE-Switch: Frequency-based Dynamic LoRA Switch for Style Transfer
summary: With the growing availability of open-sourced adapters trained on the same diffusion backbone for diverse scenes and objects, combining these pretrained weights enables low-cost customized generation. However, most existing model merging methods are designed for classification or text generation, and when applied to image generation, they suffer from content drift due to error accumulation across multiple diffusion steps. For image-oriented methods, training-based approaches are computationally expensive and unsuitable for edge deployment, while training-free ones use uniform fusion strategies that ignore inter-adapter differences, leading to detail degradation. We find that since different adapters are specialized for generating different types of content, the contribution of each diffusion step carries different significance for each adapter. Accordingly, we propose a frequency-domain importance-driven dynamic LoRA switch method. Furthermore, we observe that maintaining semantic consistency across adapters effectively mitigates detail loss; thus, we design an automatic Generation Alignment mechanism to align generation intents at the semantic level. Experiments demonstrate that our FREE-Switch (Frequency-based Efficient and Dynamic LoRA Switch) framework efficiently combines adapters for different objects and styles, substantially reducing the training cost of high-quality customized generation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FREE-Switch, a novel framework for efficiently combining open-sourced diffusion adapters for customized image generation, minimizing training costs and avoiding content drift or detail degradation.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in image generation caused by content drift and detail degradation when merging pretrained adapters for diverse objects and styles, while also minimizing computational expense.
AI.method: The authors propose a frequency-domain importance-driven dynamic LoRA switch paired with a semantic-alignment mechanism to combine pretrained diffusion adapters more effectively.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that FREE-Switch achieves high-quality customized generation while substantially reducing training cost and maintaining detail quality.
AI.conclusion: FREE-Switch represents an efficient solution for merging adapters in open-sourced diffusion models, combining diverse generation tasks with reduced computational requirements and improved detail preservation.

title: WOODELF-HD: Efficient Background SHAP for High-Depth Decision Trees
summary: Decision-tree ensembles are a cornerstone of predictive modeling, and SHAP is a standard framework for interpreting their predictions. Among its variants, Background SHAP offers high accuracy by modeling missing features using a background dataset. Historically, this approach did not scale well, as the time complexity for explaining n instances using m background samples included an O(mn) component. Recent methods such as Woodelf and PLTreeSHAP reduce this to O(m+n), but introduce a preprocessing bottleneck that grows as 3^D with tree depth D, making them impractical for deep trees. We address this limitation with WoodelfHD, a Woodelf extension that reduces the 3^D factor to 2^D. The key idea is a Strassen-like multiplication scheme that exploits the structure of Woodelf matrices, reducing matrix-vector multiplication from O(k^2) to O(k*log(k)) via a fully vectorized, non-recursive implementation. In addition, we merge path nodes with identical features, reducing cache size and memory usage. When running on standard environments, WoodelfHD enables exact Background SHAP computation for trees with depths up to 21, where previous methods fail due to excessive memory usage. For ensembles of depths 12 and 15, it achieves speedups of 33x and 162x, respectively, over the state-of-the-art.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WoodelfHD, an improved algorithm for computing Background SHAP values for decision-tree ensembles, addressing scalability issues and demonstrating significant performance improvements over prior methods.
AI.motivation: To address the scalability limitations in computing Background SHAP values for deep decision-tree ensembles, particularly overcoming the O(mn) complexity and the preprocessing bottleneck scaling as 3^D with tree depth.
AI.method: The proposed method, WoodelfHD, optimizes Background SHAP computation by introducing a novel Strassen-like matrix multiplication for Woodelf matrices, reducing operations from O(k^2) to O(k*log(k)), along with memory optimizations like merging identical path nodes.
AI.result: WoodelfHD enables exact Background SHAP computations for trees with depths up to 21, achieving dramatic speedups of 33x and 162x for tree depths 12 and 15 over state-of-the-art methods and reducing memory issues.
AI.conclusion: WoodelfHD effectively extends the applicability of Background SHAP to deeper trees, resolving prior scalability bottlenecks and improving both computational efficiency and memory usage.

title: TInR: Exploring Tool-Internalized Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) has emerged as a promising direction by extending Large Language Models' (LLMs) capabilities with external tools during reasoning. Existing TIR methods typically rely on external tool documentation during reasoning. However, this leads to tool mastery difficulty, tool size constraints, and inference inefficiency. To mitigate these issues, we explore Tool-Internalized Reasoning (TInR), aiming at facilitating reasoning with tool knowledge internalized into LLMs. Achieving this goal presents notable requirements, including tool internalization and tool-reasoning coordination. To address them, we propose TInR-U, a tool-internalized reasoning framework for unified reasoning and tool usage. TInR-U is trained through a three-phase pipeline: 1) tool internalization with a bidirectional knowledge alignment strategy; 2) supervised fine-tuning warm-up using high-quality reasoning annotations, and 3) reinforcement learning with TInR-specific rewards. We comprehensively evaluate our method across in-domain and out-of-domain settings. Experiment results show that TInR-U achieves superior performance in both settings, highlighting its effectiveness and efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Tool-Internalized Reasoning (TInR-U) framework to improve reasoning efficiency by internalizing tool knowledge into Large Language Models.
AI.motivation: Existing methods of Tool-Integrated Reasoning face challenges such as tool mastery difficulty and inefficiencies due to reliance on external documentation.
AI.method: TInR-U employs a three-phase training pipeline: knowledge alignment for tool internalization, fine-tuning with reasoning annotations, and reinforcement learning with specific rewards.
AI.result: Experiments show TInR-U's superior performance in both in-domain and out-of-domain scenarios, showcasing its reasoning effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Internalizing tool knowledge into LLMs enhances reasoning capabilities and operational efficiency, making TInR-U a promising approach for tool interaction challenges.

title: Gypscie: A Cross-Platform AI Artifact Management System
summary:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) models, encompassing both traditional machine learning (ML) and more advanced approaches such as deep learning and large language models (LLMs), play a central role in modern applications. AI model lifecycle management involves the end-to-end process of managing these models, from data collection and preparation to model building, evaluation, deployment, and continuous monitoring. This process is inherently complex, as it requires the coordination of diverse services that manage AI artifacts such as datasets, dataflows, and models, all orchestrated to operate seamlessly.
